Tafseer:
Address to the Hasty Ones and Their Warning: Whenever the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) informed his people of the reality that 'if you do not adopt the path of truth to which I invite you, then after the period of respite passes, Allah's punishment will fall upon you,' the response from the rebellious people would be: 'Why do you not bring that punishment with which you are threatening us? We will believe your word only when we see that punishment of which you warn us day in and day out.' Its detail will come ahead in verse 33. Addressing these hasty ones, it is stated that the command of Allah for the punishment has already been issued, so do not seek to hasten it. 'The command of Allah has come' is not merely a threat, but is the statement of a reality. We have clarified this divine sunnah (way) under several verses in this Book—showing that the very advent of a messenger among a nation implies that those who believe in him will find salvation, and those who deny him will be destroyed. The messenger is the touchstone for distinguishing between truth and falsehood, and is the final means of the completion of proof. For this reason, after the advent of a messenger, only two paths remain for his nation: either they believe in him and attain salvation, or they fall into the grip of God and face the worst consequence of their rebellion. 'Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him.' That is, these people must not remain under the illusion that those whom they have made partners and intercessors with God will save them from God's punishment. God is pure and far above their assumed partners. He has no partner. The sublime attributes with which He is characterized hold no compatibility with these polytheistic superstitions. He is unique in all His attributes and is One, without partner.

A Style of Eloquence: Note also this difference in the style of expression in this verse: in 'do not seek to hasten it' (fala tasta'jilooh), they are addressed directly, but in 'above what they associate' (amma yushrikoon), the third-person plural pronoun (absent form) is used instead of direct address. The eloquence in this is that the first segment contains a threat and warning, for which the style of direct address is more appropriate; whereas this second segment contains the expression of disgust and aversion, for which the third-person form is more suitable—as if the statement was made turning away from them.

Reply to Some Demands of the Disbelievers: The demand of the disbelievers was for two things. First, that angels should descend upon them in the same manner as the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) claims they descend upon him. Second, for that punishment with which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) threatened them. Consequently, reference to both these demands of theirs is in this very Surah: 'Do they await except that the angels should come to them or there comes the command of your Lord?' The answer to the second demand was given in the previous verse: the command of Allah has already been issued for the punishment, so do not seek to hasten it. Now, this is the reply to their first demand—showing that not every person is worthy of the descent of angels upon him. Allah sends His angels upon whichever of His servants He wills; that is, those whom He finds worthy of it and whom He selects for this purpose. 'With the inspiration of His command'—meaning that these angels descend with the 'spirit' (Ar-Rooh) of Allah's command. 'Spirit' refers to divine revelation. Divine revelation is termed as 'spirit' because just as the physical life of the body is by the spirit, the life of the spirit and heart is by divine revelation. Jesus (peace be upon him) stated that man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God.

The Common Instruction of Allah to the Messengers: '...[saying], "Warn that there is no deity except Me, so fear Me."' That is, every messenger received this instruction from Allah: warn the people that there is no deity besides Me, so fear Me alone and worship Me alone. Do not associate anyone else as a partner with Me.

'He created the heavens and the earth in truth. High is He above what they associate with Him.'

The Necessary Implication of the Universe Being Created in Truth: The explanation of the word 'truth' (Al-Haqq) has passed under verse 85 of Surah Al-Hijr. It means purpose and goal. The meaning is that God did not create this world without a purpose or goal. It is not a game of an amusement-seeker or a playground of children; rather, the power and wisdom apparent in every single thing of it bear witness that its Creator is Wise and All-Powerful. It is unbecoming of a Wise, All-Powerful Creator to perform a useless, vain, and purposeless work. Its being purposeful and goal-oriented necessarily requires that He bring a Day when all return to Him and receive the reward or punishment of their deeds. If this does not happen, then this entire universe is reduced to a useless, purposeless play. This reality is clarified in verse 115 of Surah Al-Mu'minoon: 'Then did you think that We created you uselessly and that to Us you would not be returned?' 'High is He above what they associate with Him'—this is an emphasis of the above subject from another perspective. The disbelievers and polytheists, firstly, did not believe in the Resurrection; moreover, their actual relationship remained with their imaginary deities, partners, and intercessors whom they worshipped. Their assumption was that these deities were sufficient to deal with God on their behalf. Obviously, this belief is an explicit negation of the universe being created in truth. For this reason, this reality is also clarified: none of those deities to whom these people are devoted is going to be of any help to them. God has no partner or associate; He is pure and free from all these partners. The sublime attributes with which He is characterized hold no compatibility with these partners.

'He created man from a sperm-drop; then at once, he is a clear adversary.' 'Man' here refers to the disbelievers and polytheists addressed in the verses above. To express aversion toward them, the statement is put in a general form.

The meaning is: We created man from a drop of insignificant water, but now he has stood up as an open adversary to Us! Now, he considers his being raised again as something impossible, saying: 'When we die and become dust—that is a far-fetched return.' And he also disputes with Us in defense of those whom he has associated as partners with Us according to his assumption. His disputing is referred to ahead in verse 27: 'And He will say, "Where are My partners for whose sake you used to oppose?"'

'And the grazing livestock He has created; for you in them is warmth and numerous benefits, and from them you eat.' 'Dif'' refers to the hair and wool of cattle, from which clothing is made to obtain warmth in winters.

Indication of the Divine Signs: Now, in this verse and the subsequent verses, reference is made to the things surrounding the addressed group and their diverse benefits to draw their attention to the fact that every single thing bears witness that the Creator of this universe is highly Benevolent, Wise, Merciful, and Compassionate. The meaning is: you received all blessings from God, yet you worship others; and regarding Him Whose acts of sustenance you witness, you assume that He has merely left you to enjoy these blessings, as if He has no right upon you in return for these blessings, and as if you will never have to face any accountability before Him. Among the blessings, cattle are mentioned first. The reason is that cattle were the primary wealth of the Arabs. They obtained clothing, food, and various other benefits mostly from them.

'And for you in them is beauty when you bring them in [and] when you send them out to pasture.' 'Beauty' (jamal) here refers to prestige, status, wealth, and grandeur. Since cattle were the primary wealth of the Arabs, as we indicated, a person's wealth and prestige were estimated by his herd. If his herd was large, he was considered a major person; and if it was small, he was considered a minor person. 'Irahah' means returning the herd from the pasture to the home in the evening, and 'sarh' means sending it out in the morning to graze. Here, 'irahah' (bringing in) is placed before 'sarh' (sending out) because the context is one of expressing prestige, and prestige is manifested more when the herd returns in the evening, having grazed, looking fresh and fat. This is not the case to the same degree when it is sent out to graze in the morning.

'And they carry your loads to a land you could not have reached except with difficulty to yourselves. Indeed, your Lord is Compassionate and Merciful.' This points to camels, upon which transport and travel in Arabia entirely depended. This animal holds the status of a divine ship for long and arduous journeys, particularly in desert and hot countries. No other animal can match it in this attribute. 'Indeed, your Lord is Compassionate and Merciful'—this is the actual lesson that the human being enjoying these blessings should obtain: he should acknowledge that the Bestower of these blessings is highly Compassionate, Merciful, and Benevolent, and then adopt what necessarily follows from this—that is, acknowledge the right of this Benefactor, become His grateful servant, remain active in His worship and obedience, do not associate others as partners with Him, and do not stand up as an adversary to Him. But it is a strange misfortune of man that, upon receiving God's blessings, instead of becoming His grateful servant, he falls into self-pride and stands up as an adversary to God. Even worse: if he is grateful, he is grateful not to God, but to others besides God.

'And [He created] the horses, mules and donkeys for you to ride and [as] adornment. And He creates that which you do not know.' After the camel, this points to other animals which serve as rides, and—being among the requirements of leadership—were also a source of prestige and grandeur. It is also stated that it is not limited to these; God also creates countless creatures of which you do not even know, but from which you or others besides you benefit directly or indirectly. The purpose of mentioning these things is also the same as indicated above—showing that all blessings are bestowed by God, yet upon receiving them, you forget God and engage in self-pride and the worship of others.

'And upon Allah is the direction of the [straight] path, and among them are those that are deviant. And if He willed, He could have guided you all.'

'Qasd' means straight and direct. 'Tariq-e-qasd' means a straight path; in 'qasdas-sabeel', the adjective is appended to its described noun. The meaning is: the straight path of monotheism leads the servant to God. There are no crooked ways or winding pathways in it. God has not placed intermediaries between Him and His servants. He has opened a straight path to reach Him; if the servant adopts it, it leads him directly to God. This reality has been clarified in verse 41 of Surah Al-Hijr: 'This is a path [leading] to Me which is straight.' The same theme has been stated in Surah Hud, verse 56: 'Indeed, my Lord is on a straight path.' 'And among them are those that are deviant'—that is, the straight path of monotheism leads to God, but people, due to their misfortune, have carved out crooked paths of polytheism from this straight path, walking on which they get so lost that they keep moving further away from God, making it extremely difficult for them to return to the main highway. 'And if He willed, He could have guided you all'—that is, if God wanted, He could have put everyone on the path of guidance by force, but He did not prefer compulsion in this matter; rather, He gave people choice so they may use their intellect and choose whichever path they want on their own responsibility. If they choose the path of monotheism, they will reach the destination; and if they deviate from it, they will see its consequence for themselves. It is stated ahead in this Surah: 'If you are eager for their guidance, indeed, Allah does not guide those He sends astray, and they have no helpers.' That is, those upon whom God's law of misguidance is implemented do not receive guidance; so there is no need to exhaust oneself over the guidance of such people.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 10 to 23: The Theme of Monotheism from Another Perspective: In the subsequent verses, this very theme of monotheism is clarified from another perspective—the perspective of harmony in the universe. Then, at the end, warning is given: those who rely on other deities should remember that on the Day of Resurrection, when people will be questioned about their deeds, these imaginary gods and goddesses of theirs are not going to be of any help. Everyone will have to deal with the One God, Who is aware of all their manifest and hidden deeds, and Who will deal with everyone strictly according to their deeds.

'It is He who sends down rain from the sky; from it is drink for you and from it is foliage in which you pasture [animals].'

'Isamah' means taking livestock to the pasture.

Proof of Monotheism from the Perspective of Harmony: The meaning of the verse is: He is the One God Who sends down water from the sky, which you who dwell on the earth drink, and from which grow those forests, bushes, and vegetation in which you pasture your livestock. Does this state of affairs testify that the God of the heavens is different and the gods of the earth are different, or does it testify that one and the same Powerful, Self-Sustaining God rules over both the heavens and the earth? Obviously, this harmony of the heavens and the earth is an open testimony that the will of a single Wise, All-Powerful Creator is active in both the heavens and the earth, and His table of sustenance is so vast that man and even the animals useful to man are benefiting from it.

'He causes to grow for you thereby the crops, olives, palm trees, grapevines, and of all the fruits. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought.'

Proof of the Hereafter from the Perspective of Sustenance: From this water, He produces crops, olives, date palms, grapevines, and gardens of diverse fruits. It is stated that in this is a very great sign for people who ponder and reflect. While the proof of monotheism from the perspective of harmony exists in it, for those who reflect, there is also a very great sign of the Hereafter in it; because when a sensitive human being ponders over this world which is full of diverse blessings and wisdom, he spontaneously exclaims: 'Our Lord, You did not create this in vain.' Its being purposeful and goal-oriented necessarily requires that He bring a Day in which He rewards those who recognized their responsibilities and performed their duties in it, and holds to account those who led a life of a stray camel, devoid of intellect and hearing.

'And He subjected for you the night and the day and the sun and the moon, and the stars are subjected by His command. Indeed in that are signs for a people who reason.'

The meaning of 'subjected for you' (sakhkhara lakum), as we have clarified in other places, is that God has engaged these things in your service and for your benefit. But it is a strange thing that God engaged these things in your service so that you might become grateful to Him, and remember that He Who showed these powers and wonders for the benefit of man will not leave him irresponsible and unaccountable; yet the ignorant, instead of adopting this correct path, started worshipping these very sun, moon, and stars, forgetting the God Who is the Creator and Owner of all these things.

'And [He subjected] whatever He multiplied for you on the earth of varying colors. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who remember.'

Every Leaf is a Volume of the Creator's Recognition: That is, look also at those countless, diverse, and multicolored things that He has spread on the earth for your benefit, and ponder over them. Within them too is a very great sign for those who take heed. The meaning is: this world could have been completely flat, barren, unicolored, or even completely colorless, but it is not so; rather, its every corner is full of wonders of power. Why is it so? This is because its Creator wanted it to be a reflection and manifestation of His attributes, so that those who reflect may find some sign of their Creator's power, wisdom, mercy, and sustenance wherever they look, obtaining a lesson regarding the Artisan and His attributes from the artifact. It is as if every leaf is a volume of the Creator's recognition. At every step, there is some sign guiding toward the correct destination. The only need is that man take heed from the things that serve as reminders, not walking with closed eyes but observing everything and preserving the lessons obtained from it.

Invitation to Ponder and Remember: Note also that the capabilities of man which guide him correctly in this world are termed here in sequence as 'yatafakkaroon' (they give thought), 'ya'qiloon' (they reason), and 'yazzakkaroon' (they remember). This is a descent from the highest to the lowest. The highest attribute of man is to ponder (tafakkur) over this universe; from this pondering, he obtains guidance toward unity within this diversity, and recognition of the actual purpose of this universe. If this is not possible, at least he should use his reason (aql) and take benefit from the reminders of everything in this universe pointing toward the destination, not walking like a blind animal. Reference to these very attributes has come ahead in verses 65-67 of this Surah as 'yasma'oon' (they hear), 'ya'qiloon' (they reason), and 'yatafakkaroon' (they give thought). This is an ascent from the lowest to the highest—meaning that the minimum attribute a reasonable person must possess is that he listens to a reasonable statement and tries to understand it. If even this is not present, he is not a human at all, but merely a two-legged animal. And his highest attribute is to ponder over this universe, because from this pondering, the doors of correct knowledge and true recognition open, and the Qur'an actually wants to awaken this capability of man.

'And it is He who subjected the sea for you to eat from it tender meat and to extract from it ornaments which you wear; and you see the ships plowing through it, and that you may seek of His bounty; and perhaps you will be grateful.'

Indication of the Signs of the Sea: By subjection of the sea (taskheer-ul-bahr), it is meant what has passed above—that God has engaged the sea in your service and for your benefit, so that you obtain fresh meat from it and extract precious pearls from it for your adornment. 'And you see the ships plowing through it, and that you may seek of His bounty'—here the context is proof that before 'wa-li-tabtagoo' (and that you may seek), 'li-tarkabooha' (that you may ride them) or a verb of similar meaning is omitted, and the conjunction 'wa' points toward it. That is, you see the ships sailing in a deep and stormy sea, plowing through its water, so that you may ride them and undertake trade journeys to seek God's bounty, becoming grateful to Him for opening clear roads for you through the chest of the sea by His power and mercy, upon which your large ships pass. The purpose is: what should happen is that people show gratitude to God for this favor and blessing, but the ignorant made these rivers and seas themselves deities, starting to worship them.

Commentary on Verses 15 and 16: 'And He has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it shift with you, and rivers and roads, that you may be guided, And landmarks. And by the stars they are [also] guided.'

According to the style of high Arabic, some parts of the discourse which were clear without being made explicit are omitted here. We have unfolded them in the translation: 'rivers and roads'—meaning, He made rivers flow in it and opened roads in it; 'landmarks'—meaning, He set up landmarks for the roads.

Indication of the Signs of Earth and Heaven: God has cast the anchors of mountains within the earth to maintain its balance. He has made rivers flow in it, from which diverse benefits are obtained; He has opened roads so you may travel from one place to another, and set up various kinds of landmarks to recognize these roads. Furthermore, stars are shining in the heaven, through which people obtain guidance in determining roads, directions, and times during their journeys in deserts. The meaning is: all these things are created and bestowed by God, so worship and obedience belong to God, or to those things besides God which have created nothing?

'Then is He who creates like one who does not create? So will you not be reminded?'

Now, this brings the discourse toward those conclusions of monotheism, reward, and punishment which arise from all the blessings described above. It is said: Can He Who creates all things be like those who not only create nothing but are themselves created? 'So will you not be reminded?'—meaning, what a death of intellect that even such an obvious thing does not enter your understanding! Keep in view that the polytheists of Arabia believed in God as the Creator of all these things to which attention has been drawn above, yet they associated many of these very created things as partners with God, making them sharers in those rights which belong exclusively to the Creator, thereby treating the Creator as equal to His creation.

'And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.'

Invitation to Repentance and Reform: That is, these blessings which have been enumerated are merely a few among the countless blessings of God. If you want to count God's blessings, you cannot encompass them; yet despite this, you show ingratitude to God and associate others as partners in His rights. Due to this action of yours, you deserved that God should instantly deprive you of every blessing, but He has granted you respite because He is highly Forgiving and Merciful. He wants you to take benefit from this respite to repent and reform, becoming deserving of His mercy instead of His wrath.

'And Allah knows what you conceal and what you declare.' This is a threat and warning—meaning that although God has granted you respite due to His compassion and mercy, remember that He is fully aware of your hidden and manifest, secret and open deeds. A Day will come when nothing will remain hidden. He will take account of every grain and decide all matters with justice. This also contains a subtle indication to the reality: you know very well that all blessings are bestowed by Allah alone, but you pretend to be ignorant; your hearts know it, but your tongues deny it. This reality is stated ahead in verse 83 of this Surah: 'They recognize the favor of Allah; then they deny it, and most of them are disbelievers.'

Commentary on Verses 20 and 21: 'And those they invoke other than Allah create nothing, and they [themselves] are created. They are dead, not alive, and they do not perceive when they will be resurrected.'

The first of these two verses is general regarding all false deities, and the second verse is specific regarding those ancestors of theirs whom they worshipped. It is said that those whom they call upon besides Allah do not create anything, but they are themselves created. The meaning is: calling upon such for fulfillment of needs is mere foolishness. Then, pointing to their ancestors whom they had made deities, it is said that they are dead; they do not even know when they will be raised again. The meaning is: what is obtained by calling upon the dead? Using 'not alive' (gayru ahya') along with 'dead' (amwat) is for further emphasis—meaning, dead and completely devoid of sensation.

'Your god is one God. But those who do not believe in the Hereafter - their hearts are disapproving, and they are arrogant.'

Arrogance is the Real Cause of the Deniers' Denial: That is, this matter is an established reality—that your deity is only one God—but those who do not believe in the Hereafter, their hearts find it unpleasant and they are filled with arrogance. The meaning is: the actual reality is not hidden from these people, but since they do not believe in the Hereafter, they are fearless, and they feel it is a loss of prestige for them to abandon those things which they have been worshipping since the time of their ancestors on the word of a single person. It is not a question of whether a thing is true or false, but a question of their own pride. This arrogance is settled within them: even if a thing is completely false, since they have been practicing it, why should they abandon it and thereby confess their own falsehood through their words and actions?

'Assuredly, Allah knows what they conceal and what they declare. Indeed, He does not love the arrogant.' That is, surely Allah knows both their hidden motive for denial and their manifest denial. He knows well that the madness of arrogance is settled in their hearts, due to which they are denying the truth while knowing it to be the truth, and are clinging to falsehood while knowing it to be falsehood. So, Allah does not love such proud and arrogant ones. 'Does not love' means He holds them in wrath.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 24 to 32: In the subsequent few verses, there is first a mention of those conspiracies and mischiefs of these arrogant ones—who were mentioned above—which they were practicing to block those under their influence from the call of the Qur'an. Alongside this, there is also a mention of the end which they and their followers will face in both this world and the Hereafter as a result of their corrupt efforts. Then, in contrast, there is a mention of those righteous and God-fearing ones who were the standard-bearers of the Qur'an's call, and an indication of the reward they will receive for their efforts in this world and the Hereafter.

'And when it is said to them, "What has your Lord sent down?" they say, "Legends of the former peoples."'

'Asateer' is the plural of 'ustoorah'. It refers to baseless and fabricated stories which hold the status of mere legends.

The Reaction of the Leaders of the Quraysh to the Call of the Qur'an: The call of the Qur'an, particularly the history of the messengers and their deniers—which it presented from the most correct perspective in the most effective manner—was not something that could remain without effect. It created a stir among the common and special people of Makkah, and gradually in all of Arabia. Since the common people are free from the pride of leadership, the correct thing affects them; but they are followers of their leaders, so they want to know their opinion before making any decision. This very situation occurred in Arabia regarding the call of the Qur'an. When the call of the Qur'an and its warnings shook the people, the common people started going to their leaders, asking: 'This thing which is being presented before us as the word revealed by God, what is it? What is your opinion about it? And the end of denial described in it, in support of which historical events are also presented, what is its reality in your view?' The leaders, realizing that their followers were being affected by it and that this could pose a danger to their leadership, started giving this reply to satisfy them: 'What else is in it except the legends of the former peoples and stories of the past?' Meaning that the baseless stories of 'Aad, Thamud, and the ancient past are being repeated in it in its own style. There is no need to be frightened by these stories; our way is completely correct, and we are on the correct religion of our ancestors. Do not be duped by this person who is presenting his fabricated statements in the name of God.

'That they may bear their burdens [i.e., sins] in full on the Day of Resurrection and some of the burdens of those whom they misguide without knowledge. Unquestionably, evil is that which they bear.'

This describes the end of their futile efforts to block people from the truth. It is said that the consequence of what they are doing is that on the Day of Resurrection, they will bear the full burden of their own misguidance, because on that Day, those partners and intercessors whom they associated with God are not going to be of any help to them. Moreover, according to the measure of their corrupt efforts, they will also have to bear a portion of the burdens of those who were misguided by their corrupt efforts. In 'And some of the burdens of those whom they misguide...', 'min' (some/of) is partitive, clarifying the reality that no person will be completely absolved of his responsibility on the Day of Resurrection on the basis that someone else misguided him; rather, he too will have to face the consequence of his misguidance. The reason is that a person who is blessed with intellect and understanding and is subject to accountability cannot present the excuse that because someone else misguided him, he became misguided. After all, why did he not use the intellect and insight that Allah had granted him? For this reason, just as his misguider will be responsible for his misguidance to his own extent and will face punishment, similarly, this person too will be held responsible for his misguidance to his own extent and will face its consequence. Through 'without knowledge' (bi-gayri ilm), the gravity of their crime is highlighted: on one side is the call of the Messenger which is based on the true knowledge revealed from Allah, and on the other side are these mischief-makers who have stood up to guide people without any knowledge, throwing them into the pit of ruin.

'Those before them had already planned, but Allah came at [i.e., uprooted] their building from the foundations, and the roof fell upon them from above them, and the punishment came to them from where they did not perceive.'

'Makr' means conspiracy and trickery. Since all these activities of the mischief-makers were merely to protect their leadership, and they were knowingly opposing the truth, the Qur'an termed it as 'makr'.

This refers to those nations of the past who opposed their messengers under the same motives under which the leaders of the Quraysh are opposing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). It is said that as a punishment, Allah's wrath uprooted their buildings from their foundations, and the roof fell upon them from above, and Allah's punishment fell upon them from where they could not even assume. When Allah's messengers warned them of Allah's punishment, they, due to their comfortable conditions, would be surprised as to from where punishment could fall upon them. Ultimately, when the punishment came, they realized that this was its path of arrival. Note that this is not merely a metaphor, but is the statement of a physical event: the punished nations were destroyed by earthquakes, fierce winds, and floods, causing the walls and roofs of their houses to fall upon them.

'Then on the Day of Resurrection He will disgrace them and say, "Where are My partners for whose sake you used to oppose?" Those who were given knowledge will say, "Indeed disgrace, this Day, and evil are upon the disbelievers."

'Khiz_y' means disgrace and humiliation. Since the root of all the corruption of these mischief-makers, as mentioned above, was arrogance, Allah will humiliate and disgrace them on the Day of Resurrection as a punishment for this arrogance of theirs. 'And He will say, "Where are My partners for whose sake you used to oppose?"'—this describes a form of their disgrace and humiliation: Allah will ask them to show where His partners are in whose defense they were always ready to fight. Its detail comes ahead in verse 86: 'And when those who associated partners with Allah see their partners, they will say, "Our Lord, these are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You." But they will throw back at them the word: "Indeed, you are liars."' Obviously, this will be the height of their disgrace—that those in whose defense they stood against God and His Messenger throughout their lives will call them liars at the exact moment of need.

'Those who were given knowledge will say, "Indeed disgrace, this Day, and evil are upon the disbelievers."' The mischief-makers who stood up to misguide people without any knowledge were mentioned above. Now, in contrast, this mentions those who received a share from the knowledge bestowed by Allah, illuminating themselves and guiding others in its light. It is said that when these people see this disgrace of the standard-bearers of polytheism, they will exclaim with joy: 'Today, disgrace and punishment are the share of the disbelievers.' Meaning that they enjoyed themselves in the world, but today they will see the end of this arrogant self-indulgence.

Commentary on Verses 28 and 29: 'The ones whom the angels take in death while they are wronging themselves. Then they will show submission, [saying], "We were not doing any evil." Yes! Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what you used to do. So enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and wretched is the residence of the arrogant.'

Application of the Discourse to the Present State: These two verses serve as a parenthetical application. This clarifies the statement of the people of knowledge—showing that it is not merely a story of the future, but applies exactly to those who are currently intoxicated with pride and die in this state. 'The ones whom the angels take in death while they are wronging themselves'—meaning that under the category of these disbelievers are included all those whose souls the angels extract while they are wronging their own selves due to their disbelief and polytheism. 'Then they will show submission, [saying], "We were not doing any evil."' 'Salam' is a noun from 'tasleem', meaning surrender and submission. 'Alqawus-salam' means they will surrender, drop their guards, and sue for peace. That is, all this pride, arrogance, and pomp of theirs lasts only until they face the angels of death. When they face them, and the angels extract their souls while striking their faces and backs—as mentioned in another place—the intoxication vanishes in the very first stage, and they make requests with great humility, presenting false excuses: 'Have mercy on us; we were not doing any evil.' 'Yes! Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what you used to do'—the angels reply: 'Yes, you were doing evil; indeed, Allah is fully aware of what you used to do.' 'So enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and wretched is the residence of the arrogant'—meaning that they are informed at that very moment: 'Now enter through the gates of Hell to remain in it forever; escaping from it will never be possible.' This is the abode of the arrogant, and it is a wretched abode. The word 'gates' (abwab) is plural, pointing to the vastness of Hell, and showing that the criminals will enter through different gates according to the nature and gravity of their crimes.

'And it will be said to those who feared Allah, "What has your Lord sent down?" They will say, "[That which is] good." For those who do good in this world is good; and the home of the Hereafter is better. And excellent is the home of the righteous.'

The Attitude of the Righteous in Contrast to the Arrogant: Now, this describes the attitude of those who feared God, in contrast to the arrogant. It is said that when they are asked what God has revealed, they praise it highly, saying: 'He has revealed that which is excellent.' 'For those who do good...'—this describes the reward of these righteous and good-doing people which they will receive in both this world and the Hereafter. It is said that for them is goodness and success in this world, and what can be said of the Hereafter—it is indeed far better. 'And excellent is the home of the righteous' stands directly in contrast to 'and wretched is the residence of the arrogant'. Just as there is no limit to the wretchedness of the arrogant ones' residence, there is no limit to the excellence of the righteous ones' home.

Here, in clear words, is a glad tiding of success and victory in this world as well for these righteous ones. The reason, as we have clarified in another place, is that victory and authority in this world are guaranteed for the Messenger and his companions. We will clarify this further under verse 41.

'Gardens of perpetual residence, which they will enter, beneath which rivers flow. They will have therein whatever they wish. Thus does Allah reward the righteous.'

The Meaning of 'Gardens of Perpetual Residence' (Jannatu Adn): 'Adn' means residence and permanent stay. This is the description of the righteous ones' home—showing that they will be gardens of permanent residence. The righteous servants of God will enter them not merely for temporary recreation, but to dwell in them forever. The verb 'yadkhuloonaha' (they will enter them) is in its true and perfect meaning here—showing that the righteous will be seated in these gardens with honor and respect, and whatever they desire will be present for them. 'Beneath which rivers flow' is the depiction and praise of these gardens. The concept of an excellent garden is that it is situated on an elevation with a river flowing beneath it. The elevation doubles its beauty, and the river flowing beneath guarantees its freshness and fertility.

'The ones whom the angels take in death, [being] good, saying, "Peace be upon you. Enter Paradise for what you used to do."

The Angels' Treatment of the Righteous: Just as verse 28 above serves as a parenthetical application, this verse also serves as a parenthetical application, making the discourse connect exactly with the present state. 'Good' (tayyibeen) stands directly in contrast to 'wronging themselves' (zalimee anfusihim). The meaning is: these righteous ones refer to those whom the angels take in death while they are completely pure and free from every trial of polytheism and disbelief. The angels greet them with peace and give them glad tidings of Paradise in reward for their deeds.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 33 to 47: Refutation of the Arrogant Ones' Sophistry and Glad Tiding of Success to the Believers: In the subsequent verses, there is a reference to and refutation of the sophistries which these arrogant ones presented in defense of their attitude. At the same time, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions are exhorted to steadfastness and trust in Allah, and are assured that after passing through the period of trials, ultimate victory and success belong to them alone. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 33 and 34: 'Do they [i.e., the disbelievers] await except that the angels should come to them or there comes the command of your Lord? Thus did those before them. And Allah wronged them not, but they had been wronging themselves. So the evil consequences of what they did struck them, and they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule.'

Invitation to Obtain a Lesson from History: That is, these arrogant ones are not ready to believe your words until the angels come to them or that punishment with which they are being warned falls upon them. 'Thus did those before them'—meaning that their predecessor nations adopted this very path with their messengers, and the result was that Allah's punishment fell upon them and destroyed them. The same fate will be theirs if they do not change their path. 'And Allah wronged them not, but they had been wronging themselves'—meaning that these past nations perished due to their own stubbornness and rebellion; God did not commit any injustice against them, as He had already informed them of all the results and consequences; rather, they wronged their own selves by demanding punishment instead of taking benefit from warnings and admonitions, and by mocking the warnings of their messengers. 'So the evil consequences of what they did struck them...'—meaning that the punishments that fell upon them were the results of their own deeds. God showed them exactly what they had prepared for themselves.

'And those who associated partners with Allah said, "If Allah had willed, we would not have worshipped anything other than Him, neither we nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything other than Him." Thus did those before them. Then is there upon the messengers except clear notification?'

Reply to Some Sophistries of the Polytheists: This is also an example of their sophistry. That is, these polytheists, to corner you, say: 'Whatever deities we worship and whatever things we declare forbidden in their names, we do all this by God's will; otherwise, everything is in God's power, and if these actions of ours were against His will, He would have stopped us from these actions by His power, and we would not have been able to perform even a single one of these actions.' 'Thus did those before them'—meaning that this too is nothing new; the nations that passed before them also tried to silence their messengers through similar sophistries. The meaning is: since they have adopted their path, they will surely face their end too. 'Then is there upon the messengers except clear notification?'—meaning that if they consider the proof of your truthfulness to be that you use your power to force them onto the path of religion to which you invite them, then this is not the task of the messengers. Their task is solely to convey the religion of truth to people clearly; you are performing this task, and your responsibility ends there. Accepting the truth or not accepting it is their own responsibility, and they will be held accountable for it before God. It is not the way of Allah to force people onto truth or falsehood; rather, He has granted people choice. If a person chooses the path of truth, he is guided to it; and if a person walks on the path of falsehood, Allah grants him respite in it.

'And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid Taghut." And among them were those whom Allah guided, and among them were those upon whom error was deservedly decreed. So proceed through the earth and observe how was the end of the deniers.'

The explanation of the word 'Taghut' has passed under verse 256 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Every Messenger of Allah Invited to the Worship of Allah Alone: The meaning of the verse is: if the intent behind their statement is that Allah's messengers taught the same disbelief and polytheism on which they stand, then this is completely contrary to the facts. Every messenger of Allah taught only His worship and the avoidance of Taghut; so whoever chose the path of guidance was guided, and whoever remained obstinate in his misguidance became completely overcome by misguidance. 'So proceed through the earth...'—meaning that the ruins of the end of those who denied these messengers exist in this land, so observe these ruins and obtain a lesson from them.

'[Even] if you should strive for their guidance, indeed, Allah does not guide those He sends astray, and they have no helpers.'

Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): This is consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). You had an intense desire for the guidance of your people, due to which you exhausted yourself day and night for them. Allah consoled you: those who have fallen under the grip of God's law of misguidance cannot receive guidance. No one becomes a helper to such. God grants guidance only to those who use their natural capabilities for it and seek it.

'And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths that Allah will not resurrect one who dies. Yes! [It is] a promise binding upon Him in truth, but most of the people do not know.'

'And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths'—meaning they went to extremes in swearing oaths. Among the hindrances to faith for the arrogant of the Quraysh, a major one was that they did not believe in life after death, nor did they want to believe in it. They would swear strong oaths to satisfy those under their influence: 'Being raised again after death is impossible; Allah will not raise anyone. This is merely a threat from Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).' 'Yes! [It is] a promise binding upon Him in truth...'—this is their refutation, and this refutation carries the same intensity as the statement of the deniers. It is said: yes, He will surely raise people. This is Allah's definitive promise which He has made binding upon Himself, but most people are unaware of this; for this reason, they deny it or make fun of it.

'[It is] so He may make clear to them that wherein they differ and that those who have disbelieved may know that they were liars.'

This clarifies the need for being raised again after death—showing that this will happen so that the truth may be clearly explained regarding those things in which they are differing today, and those who knowingly committed excesses may see the end of their deeds with their own eyes. 'And that those who have disbelieved may know that they were liars'—and this will also happen so that those who are today swearing oaths to satisfy people that there is no Resurrection may know well that they were liars and swore false oaths.

'Indeed, Our only word to a thing when We intend it is that We say to it, "Be," and it is.'

This removes the assumption of impossibility on the basis of which the disbelievers denied the Resurrection. It is said: when We intend to perform any task, We do not need to make elaborate arrangements; We merely say 'Be,' and that task is performed; so what is difficult for Us in raising people again?

Commentary on Verses 41 and 42: 'And those who emigrated for [the cause of] Allah after they had been wronged - We will surely settle them in this world in a good place; but the reward of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew. [They are] those who endured patiently and upon their Lord they rely.'

Appreciation of the Emigrants to Abyssinia: This is also stated here in connection with the need for and purposes of the Resurrection—meaning that, after all, there are also those servants of God who are today targeted with various types of calamities and hardships in His way, for the sake of His religion, to the extent that they were forced to leave their beloved homeland and homes merely to save their religion, migrating to a foreign land like Abyssinia. Will Allah not reward these people for their sacrifices? Surely He will. He will settle them well in this world too, and the reward of the Hereafter is far greater than this. 'If they only knew'—meaning that those who are unaware of the value of this promise of Allah and the grandeur of the reward of the Hereafter may think whatever they wish; would that they knew! If they knew, they would realize what a great success in both this world and the Hereafter has been achieved by those whom they consider deprived. '[They are] those who endured patiently and upon their Lord they rely'—this is the praise of these emigrants: they bore all kinds of hardships in the way of Allah but did not waver; rather, they remained firm on their stance of truth, and when it came to leaving their homes, they left relying on Allah, caring nothing for what they would eat and where they would shelter.

The Reality of Emigration (Hijrah): From these verses, the reality of Hijrah also becomes clear—showing that not every relocation of home is Hijrah. Hijrah is when a person is persecuted regarding his religion, to the extent that he is forced to leave his beloved homeland and home, and seek refuge in another land. Patience (sabr) in this path means that—even if saws are run through one's head—one is not ready to surrender the blessing of the religion of truth which one has received; and trust (tawakkul) means that no matter how unfavorable the conditions are, one maintains trust that Allah will not leave him alone, but will support him. This patience and trust serve as the provision for the journey of Hijrah.

'And We sent not before you with revelation except men; so ask the people of the message [i.e., former scriptures] if you do not know.'

Reply to Those Awaiting the Punishment: This is the reply to those whose mention has passed in verse 33—that they are waiting for the angels to come to them or for Allah's punishment to fall upon them. First, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it is said: all the messengers sent before you were human; the only distinction they possessed was that We revealed Our message to them. Then, addressing the objectors, it is said: if you are unaware of this reality, ask the previous bearers of the Book; they will tell you that only humans have been appointed to the office of messengership among humans, not angels.

'[We sent them] with clear proofs and written ordinances. And We revealed to you the message [i.e., the Quran] that you may make clear to the people what was sent down to them and that they might give thought.'

'With clear proofs and written ordinances'—meaning that these messengers who came used to come with proofs and scriptures; they did not come bringing the divine punishment which these people are demanding. 'And We revealed to you the message...'—meaning that just as We sent previous messengers with proofs and scriptures, We have similarly revealed Our message (the Book) to you so that you may clearly explain to people that which has been revealed to them, and so that the differences in which they fell regarding their religion may be resolved. Cast a glance back at verse 39. 'And that they might give thought'—meaning that the purpose of revealing the Book is also that they reflect on it so that their crooked ways and misguidance are reformed. If the punishment fell upon them, the opportunity for this would not remain. It is a very great favor and blessing of Allah that He sent the Book instead of the punishment, which is a light for resolving differences and a guide for intellect; so they ought to be grateful to their Lord for this blessing, believe in it, obtain guidance from it, and not seek to hasten the punishment.

Commentary on Verses 45 to 47: 'Then do those who program evil deeds feel secure that Allah will not cause the earth to swallow them or that the punishment will not come upon them from where they do not perceive? Or that He will not seize them during their activity, and they are not causing failure [to Him]? Or that He will not seize them upon gradual decay? Indeed, your Lord is Compassionate and Merciful.'

Expression of Astonishment at the Demand for Punishment: This is an expression of astonishment and blame at their demand for punishment—showing that if they are demanding punishment, on what basis are they doing so? God can seize them from wherever He wills. What arrangements have they made to escape His seizure? If God wills, He can cause the earth to swallow them along with themselves, or His punishment can fall upon them from where they do not even assume; or if He wills, He can seize them during their travels, and even then they cannot escape His control. Similarly, if He wills, He can seize them at the very moment when they are feeling the danger and are alert to it. In short, He can seize them under all conditions; if He does not seize them, it is because He is Compassionate and Merciful. He grants respite to His servants to the ultimate limit and overlooks them.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 48 to 60: Cosmic and Psychological Proofs of Monotheism: In the subsequent verses, some cosmic and psychological proofs of monotheism are described, and the purpose of describing them is to emphasize the warning contained in verses 45-47 above. The meaning is: do not seek to hasten the punishment; if God's punishment fell, no one besides God can save you from it. If anyone relies on their gods and goddesses, let it be clear that God has no partner. He is One, without partner. No one has the power to interfere with His will.

'Do they not see what things Allah has created? Their shadows incline to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah while they are humble.'

'Tafayyu' means turning and shifting.

This is a physical proof of monotheism—showing that the shadow of everything lies stretched on the earth, as if it is in prostration before God. Then, it slowly begins to rise until when the sun comes to the meridian, the shadow stands straight; then, when the sun begins to decline, the shadow begins to stretch in the opposite direction on the earth. Twenty-four hours of the shadow of everything pass in this bowing and prostration, and it is also worthy of attention that this prostration of the shadow is always in the direction opposite to the sun: if the sun is in the east, the shadow will stretch toward the west; and if the sun is in the west, the shadow will stretch toward the east. This is a subtle indication to the reality that the nature of the shadow is Abrahamic. It is averse to sun worship.

Ponder in the light of this physical testimony, and you will realize that if any person worships the sun or any of the stars, his own shadow refutes this action of his. He himself bows before the sun, but his shadow bows in the opposite direction. He who is not willing to prostrate to God of his own choice (taw'an) is prostrating to Him by force (karhan) because his shadow is bowed before God alone. Verse 15 of Surah Ar-Ra'd points to this reality: 'And to Allah prostrates whoever is within the heavens and the earth, willingly or by compulsion, and their shadows in the mornings and the afternoons.'

The meaning is: the one who prostrates to God of his own choice has perfect compatibility between his physical prostration and his chosen prostration; on the other hand, the one who prostrates to other than God finds that his own shadow refutes this prostration of his. 'While they are humble'—meaning that in this physical prostration, humility and helplessness overcome them. Now, if any person acts arrogantly toward God in this state, it means that he is deviating from his own basic nature within his sphere of choice.

'And to Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of creatures, and the angels [as well], and they are not arrogant.'

The Humility and Obedience of the Angels: That is, all living creatures in the heavens and the earth prostrate to God alone, because the basic nature of all is God-worship. The angels also prostrate to God alone. Let the ignorant make them whatever they wish, but they are obedient servants of God; they do not act arrogantly toward Him, but comply with His every command without question.

'They fear their Lord above them, and they do what they are commanded.' That is, despite all their proximity to God, their position is far below the high presence of God. They remain constantly in fear of God above them. They never fall into the trials of pride or insolence, but comply with every command immediately.

'And Allah has said, "Do not take for yourselves two deities. He is only one God, so fear only Me."

God's Testimony to Monotheism: This refers to God's own testimony to monotheism which has reached people through His prophets, messengers, Books, and scriptures—showing that He taught people through His prophets and messengers: 'Do not make two deities; indeed, I am only one God, so fear Me alone.'

'And to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him is [due] obedience constantly. Then is it other than Allah that you fear?'

'Deen' here means obedience, and 'wasib' means constant. The meaning is: whatever is in the heavens and the earth belongs to God alone. He alone is deserving of obedience constantly; that is, in this world too, His obedience must be practiced, and in the Hereafter too, His obedience will be practiced. If you fear anyone other than God, this is entirely your ignorance.

'And whatever you have of favor - it is from Allah. Then when adversity touches you, to Him you cry for help.' 'Ja'ara' means to humble oneself and cry out.

Psychological Proof of Monotheism: This is a psychological proof of monotheism—showing that whatever blessings you possess are bestowed by Allah alone, and whenever any calamity befalls you, you call upon God alone. This is proof that within the basic nature itself, there is a consciousness of only one God; these other gods and goddesses you have set up have no place within the basic nature. Consequently, when any real trouble arrives, all these fabricated deities vanish, and only the One God remains, belief in Whom is entrusted within the basic nature.

'Then when He removes the adversity from you, at once a group of you associates partners with their Lord.' Then, when God removes the calamity, those past self-indulgences return. Those very same gods and goddesses wake up again who had vanished during the calamity, and that same reliance on means and resources is restored which previously dominated the heart and mind. Man begins to attribute the peace and comfort bestowed by God to others, consigning God Himself to oblivion.

'So they will deny what We have given them. Then enjoy yourselves, for you are going to know.' This describes the result of this situation: in this way, man shows ingratitude for the blessings bestowed by God and attributes them to others. 'Then enjoy yourselves' is a threat—meaning: show ingratitude to God and enjoy His blessings for a few days; very soon the consequence of this ingratitude will stand before you.

'And they assign to what they do not know a portion of what We have provided them. By Allah, you will surely be questioned about what you used to invent.'

Polytheism is an Invention Against God: That is, for those gods and goddesses regarding whom they possess no proof or evidence—whom they have associated as partners with God merely on the basis of illusion and assumption—they designate a portion of the blessings bestowed by God. They set aside portions in their names, and attribute many things to them, claiming they obtained them through their grace and favor. 'By Allah, you will surely be questioned...'—this is a threat with a strong oath on His own self: this invention you are fabricating against God—claiming He has made so-and-so His partner—you will be questioned about it one day; God has made no one His partner, this is merely a slander you are fabricating against Him.

'And they attribute to Allah daughters - exalted is He - and for them is what they desire.' This clarifies the double hideousness of their polytheism: firstly, it is extremely ugly to associate anyone as a partner with God; then, even worse, they attribute to God that which they dislike for themselves! For themselves they prefer sons, but for God they have designated daughters. Keep in view that the polytheists of Arabia declared the angels to be daughters of God, and worshipped them assuming that if they remained pleased, they would secure everything for them from their Father. 'Exalted is He'—meaning that Allah is pure and free from all such attributions; He has no son or daughter, all are His creatures.

Commentary on Verses 58 and 59: That is, they have attributed daughters to God, whereas regarding daughters, their own state is such that if any of them is given the news of a daughter's birth, his face turns black with grief, and he remains filled with sorrow, hiding from people because of this assumed disgrace, and wondering whether to keep her with humiliation or bury her in the earth to escape this disgrace. 'Unquestionably, evil is that which they decide'—meaning that they decide to attribute to God that which they consider extremely detestable for themselves, showing no sense of caution in attributing to God that which they detest to such a degree. How evil is the decision they made!

'For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is the evil description; and for Allah is the highest description. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'

That is, the actual reality is that those who do not believe in the Hereafter deserve the evil description, and for Allah belong the highest attributes; but these people have reversed the matter. They attribute to Allah those things which they consider bad for themselves, while claiming good results and a good end for themselves: 'And they attribute to Allah what they dislike, and their tongues assert the lie that they will have the best [outcome].'

Subsequent Subject. Verses 61 to 64: Warning to the Polytheists, Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): Subsequently, there is a warning to the polytheists: if God has granted you respite, He has done so because a time is appointed for everything with Him; when that time arrives, you can neither delay it nor hasten it. At the same time, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: what you are facing is the same as what the messengers before you faced. Your task is not to make people believers and monotheists, but solely to establish God's proof against them.

'And if Allah were to impose blame on the people for their wrongdoing, He would not have left upon it [i.e., the earth] any creature, but He defers them for a specified term. And when their term comes, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it].'

Disbelief and Polytheism is Wrongdoing (Zulm): 'Zulm' basically means violation of rights. Since polytheism and disbelief are the greatest violations of rights—by committing which the servant violates the greatest right of his Lord and also commits the greatest wrong against his own self—the Qur'an has repeatedly termed disbelief and polytheism as 'zulm'.

Reply to the Demand for Punishment: This is the reply to the disbelievers' demand for punishment—showing that it is not the way of Allah to seize people instantly for their disbelief and polytheism. If He were to do so, He would not have left even a single living creature on the earth. Rather, His way is that He grants people respite until a specified term, so that whoever wants to repent and reform may take benefit from this respite, or otherwise fill up their cup of sins. But when that specified term arrives, they get no opportunity to delay it or hasten it.

'And they attribute to Allah what they dislike, and their tongues assert the lie that they will have the best [outcome]. Assuredly, they will have the Fire, and indeed they are abandoned.' 'Afrata' means forgot and left a thing—meaning that the disbelievers and polytheists will be thrown into Hell and left abandoned in it, and no heed will be paid to them.

The Arrogance and Folly of the Polytheists: This points to their double folly: firstly, it is strange that they have attributed daughters to God, which they dislike for themselves; then, relying on them, they assume that if any stage of the Hereafter arose, everything would be well for them and success would be theirs in every stage. It is stated that for them is surely the Fire, and they will be thrown into it and abandoned forever, and no heed will be paid to them.

'By Allah, We certainly sent [messengers] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds attractive to them. So he is their ally today, and they will have a painful punishment.'

Threat to the Disbelievers, Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): This is a threat to the disbelievers and consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that what you are facing today is nothing new. This has already occurred to the messengers sent before you from their nations. We sent Our messengers so that people might adopt the path of guidance through them, but people chose Satan as their leader instead of the messengers. He made their deeds look beautiful in their eyes, and they were not ready to emerge from their misguidance. The result was that now he is their companion, and for them is a painful punishment.

'And We have not revealed to you the Book, [O Muhammad], except for you to make clear to them that wherein they have differed and as guidance and mercy for a people who believe.'

The Ultimate Limit of the Prophet's Responsibility: This describes the limit of the Prophet's responsibility—showing that We have not revealed this Book to you so that you must force people to accept it; accepting it or not accepting it is their own responsibility; your responsibility is solely to clearly explain the religion of truth in which people have differed, so that no excuse remains for them to stick to their misguidance. Keep in view that due to the intensity of his sense of duty, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) would sometimes feel that if people were not adopting the path of guidance, some shortcoming of his might be involved in it. To remove this anxiety of yours, your responsibility has been stated: your responsibility is solely to convey the religion clearly, and that is all. This subject is explained further ahead: 'And [warn of] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves. And We will bring you, [O Muhammad], as a witness over these. And We have sent down to you the Book as clarification for all things and as guidance and mercy and good tidings for the Muslims.' 'As guidance and mercy for a people who believe'—meaning that this Book is guidance in its beginning, and mercy in its end. Those who accept this guidance will ultimately deserve God's mercy.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 65 to 83: Blame upon the Polytheists, Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): Some of the blessings that Allah has bestowed are subsequently enumerated to blame the polytheists—showing which of these blessings they can attribute to anyone other than Allah. Then, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: bringing those onto the path of guidance who are knowingly pretending to be ignorant is not your responsibility; your responsibility is solely to convey the truth.

'And Allah has sent down from the sky, rain, and given life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who listen.'

Proof of Monotheism from the Perspective of Harmony: Sending down water from the heavens and reviving and fertilizing the earth after its dry and dead state is a very clear proof that the will of a single Wise, All-Powerful God is active in both the heaven and the earth. If different wills were active in them, as the polytheists assume, how could this harmony emerge, upon which the survival of this world depends?

Moreover, this is also a highly clear sign of the Resurrection and being raised again. The God Who revives the barren and dead earth after a single shower of rain, what is difficult for Him in raising people from their graves on the Day of Resurrection?

The Verb 'They Hear' (Yasma'oon) in its True Meaning: The verb 'yasma'oon' is in its true meaning here—showing that those who listen to reasonable statements with open ears, understand them, and accept them, find in this the proof of both monotheism and the Resurrection. As for those who are not ready to listen and understand, they have fallen under the grip of God's law; nothing can open the ears of such people.

'And indeed, for you in grazing livestock is a lesson. We give you drink from what is in their bellies - from between excretion and blood - pure milk, agreeable to those who drink.'

Indication of the Blessing of Milk: After the blessing of water, this points to the blessing of milk—showing that for those who reflect, there are great lessons of God's mercy, sustenance, and monotheism within cattle. These cattle graze on the grass and vegetation of this earth, which turn into dung in their bellies in one stage, and into blood in another; then, between this dung and blood, milk is produced, which contains no trace of dung or blood, but is completely pure from any mixture, and is agreeable, delicious, and nourishing for those who drink. This entire state of affairs testifies that this universe is running under the will of a single Artisan, Wise, Powerful, and Merciful God, or does it testify that different wills are working in it?

Obtaining a Lesson is the Key to Knowledge: 'Ibrah' means passing from one reality to another. This lesson is the key to knowledge. For those who possess this capability and take benefit from it, if one door opens, other doors automatically open in its light. Those who deaden this capability find that the eyes of their intellect and heart go blind; they see everything but perceive nothing.

'And from the fruits of the palm trees and grapevines you take intoxicants and good provision. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who reason.'

Indication of Some Other Signs: That is, just as He produced milk for you from cattle, He similarly provided sustenance for you from date palms and grapevines; you make intoxicants from them, and also obtain good provision. Here, by using 'good' (hasan) along with 'provision' (rizq), a subtle indication is made that making intoxicants from date palms and grapes is not their correct use, but is their misuse. Their correct use is to obtain good and healthy provision from them which gives strength to the body and intellect, not to transform them into such a form that they dull the intellect and heart.

The Wisdom of These Diversities: 'Indeed in that is a sign for a people who reason'—meaning that Allah has placed this variety and diversity in His blessings so that those who reflect may think, and those who understand may reason. Obviously, this world did not require these diversities for its survival; it could have been simple and unicolored, or even completely colorless, but its Creator wanted it to be a reflection and manifestation of His attributes so that those who reflect may think and form some concept of their Lord's high attributes, His endless power and wisdom, His unlimited compassion and mercy, His peerless sustenance, and His perfect unity, feeling the sense of their duties and responsibilities in its light.

Commentary on Verses 68 and 69: 'And your Lord inspired to the bee, "Take for yourself dwellings in the mountains and in the trees and in what they construct. Then eat from all the fruits and follow the pathways of your Lord laid easy [for you]." There emerges from their bellies a drink, varying in colors, in which there is healing for people. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought.'

'Inspiration' (Wahy) here refers to that instinctive and natural inspiration which is granted to every creature by the Creator of nature and instinct to use its inherent capabilities. 'Zulul' is the plural of 'zalool', which basically means submissive and compliant. Here, it is the adjective of 'subul' (pathways), meaning pathways that are smooth, straight, and paved.

Indication of the Blessing of Honey: After the blessing of fruits, this mentions the blessing of honey which bees prepare from these fruits. It is said that your Lord inspired the bee to make its hives in the mountains, trees, and the roofs constructed by people, then suck the nectar of all kinds of fruits, and remain active on those pathways which your Lord has laid easy for the manifestation of your capabilities. Consequently, guided by its instinct, it remains active with full energy, resulting in a drink emerging from the bellies of these bees, which varies in colors and contains healing for people's various diseases. 'Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought'—Allah manifested this power, wisdom, mercy, and sustenance of His within a single bee so that people may reflect and ponder, understand the attributes of the Creator of this universe, and reform their world and the Hereafter in the light of their requirements.

In the verses above, three attributes of those who benefit from Allah's signs have been described in sequence. One is 'yasma'oon'—meaning they hear reasonable statements with open ears and accept them; second is 'ya'qiloon'—meaning they use their reason and try to think and understand; third is 'yatafakkaroon'—meaning they constantly ponder and reflect on the secrets of the universe, striving to pass from realities to deeper realities. These attributes are the actual core of humanity, and there is a wise sequence and arrangement in them.

The first stage is 'yasma'oon'. Many realities of this universe fall under the category of obvious natural truths. To understand them, no great effort is required; a reasonable human being either understands them on his own, or since his ears are open to reasonable statements, he feels them touch his heart as soon as he hears them from another reasonable person.

The second stage is 'ya'qiloon', where there is a need to think, understand, and use reason, where premises are arranged and results are obtained. This stage is higher than the first stage, and its fruits in the path of knowledge are also more valuable, but it is within the reach of common intellect. Those who appreciate their intellect and benefit from this blessing do not remain deprived of its blessings.

The third stage is 'yatafakkaroon', which is the highest stage. This is the station of those who constantly reflect on the secrets of the universe and constantly traverse the stages of knowledge. This is the degree of the wise (hukama). Just as a bee, with its peerless effort, sucks nectar from various flowers to make honey which contains food and healing for people, similarly, these people gather the honey of wisdom through their efforts of reflection and pondering, which contains the cure for the diseases of intellect and heart; they themselves remain satisfied with it, and others also benefit from it.

'And Allah created you; then He will take you in death. And among you is he who is reversed to the most decrepit old age so that he will not know, after [having had] knowledge, a thing. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Competent.' That is, life, death, and the length or shortness of age are also from God alone. Consequently, how many among you die in childhood or youth, and how many reach the most decrepit old age, to the extent that their state becomes such that they know nothing after having had knowledge. God alone is Knowing and Competent; He is the source of all knowledge, and He alone determines measures and standards for everything. The style of expression in 'And among you is he who...' indicates that there is an omission in the preceding discourse—meaning: some among you die in childhood or youth, and some reach the most decrepit old age. This is proof that the length or shortness of age is from God alone; no one else has any choice in it. If you give due right to 'so that' (likay) in 'so that he will not know...', it shows that power wants to manifest this reality by bringing some people to the decrepit age—showing that knowledge, intellect, power, and choice are all the gift of God alone. The very human being who took great pride in his knowledge and intellect faces a time when he himself sees, and others also see, that he has become completely devoid of intellect, knowledge, power, and choice, like an infant. He depends entirely on others and is in need of them for his requirements. All his intellectual and rational capabilities return to that God Who was their actual Giver, because God alone is Knowing and Competent; whoever receives any measure of the blessing of knowledge and power receives it from God alone, so pride and arrogance over it are not justified, but gratitude for it is obligatory.

'Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Competent.'

'And Allah has favored some of you over others in provision. But those who were favored would not hand over their provision to those whom their right hands possess so they would be equal to them therein. Then is it the favor of Allah they reject?'

Sustenance is also the Gift of God: Like age, intellect, and knowledge, sustenance is also the gift of God. He alone has given less to some and more to others. This is not within anyone's own power to increase his sustenance as much as he wants. For this reason, it is the duty of every person to be grateful to God alone, and not to attribute the sustenance and favor bestowed by Allah to others, showing ingratitude to the actual Provider.

A Reality of Human Nature: 'But those who were favored would not hand over...'—this draws attention to a reality of human nature, placing the lesson obtained from it before you: your own state is such that those who possess superiority in sustenance do not distribute their wealth among their slaves to become equal to them and make them equal to themselves; so how have you assumed regarding God that He has distributed His created world among His slaves and servants, descending to their level? This is an explicit denial of God's blessings—to attribute the things He bestowed to those who had not a hair's breadth of share in creating them.

'And Allah has made for you from yourselves mates and has made for you from your mates sons and grandsons and has provided for you from the good things. Then is it in falsehood they believe and in the favor of Allah they disbelieve?'

The Blessing of Spouses and Children is from God: That is, like sustenance and favor, the blessing of spouses and children is also granted to you by God alone. He created spouses for you from your own species, and from them He initiated the lineage of sons and grandsons, and gave you good things to eat and use. The right of these blessings was that people should show gratitude to God alone and believe in Him, but the state of people is that they believe in false deities and show ingratitude to the actual Benefactor.

'And they worship besides Allah that which does not possess for them [the power of] provision from the heavens and the earth at all, and they are unable.' This details their belief in falsehood and ingratitude—showing that they worship besides Allah those things which have no power to send down any sustenance for them from the heaven, nor to produce anything from the earth. And it is not only that they do not possess this power in practice, but even if they wanted to and expended all their power for it, they could never possess this power.

'So do not assert similarities to Allah. Indeed, Allah knows and you do not know.'

Instruction to Avoid Similitudes Regarding God's Attributes: 'Asserting similarities' (darb-e-mathal) here means describing attributes for God by making comparisons and similitudes. For example, comparing Him to ourselves and saying He has daughters, or comparing Him to worldly kings and characterizing Him with attributes like theirs. Most doors of polytheism have opened through this comparison and similitude; for this reason, having given correct guidance regarding God's attributes in the verses above, the door to this trial was closed. It is said: do not make comparisons and similitudes your guide in the matter of God's attributes. God alone knows His attributes; you do not know, so accept the attributes He describes of Himself and believe in them—this is the path of guidance.

'Allah presents an example: a slave [who is] owned and cannot do anything and one to whom We have provided from Us a good provision so he spends from it secretly and publicly. Can they be equal? [All] praise to Allah! But most of them do not know.'

The Example Presented by God: That is, if you want to hear an example, God presents an example; listen to it.
