On one side is a slave who is owned, having no power over anything; and on the other side is a free man whom God has provided with good sustenance and favor, and he spends from it secretly and publicly with complete freedom. Can these two be equal? Obviously not; so how do you treat God and His owned servants as equal?

'All praise to Allah'—meaning: if they think correctly, this reality will become clear to them—that Allah alone is deserving of gratitude—but most of them are unaware of this obvious reality.

Another Example: 'And Allah presents an example of two men, one of whom is dumb and cannot do anything and he is a burden upon his guardian. Wherever he directs him, he brings no good. Is he equal to one who commands justice, while he is on a straight path?'

That is, listen to another example. There are two men, one of whom is a dumb, deaf slave, having no power over anything, and he is a burden upon his master; wherever he sends him, he does not perform any task correctly. And there is another man who is free, commands others to justice and equity, and is himself on a straight path. Can these two men be equal? If not, then how do you treat God and His helpless, powerless creatures as equal?

A Warning Parenthetically: 'And to Allah belongs the unseen of the heavens and the earth. And the command of the Hour is not but as a glance of the eye or even nearer. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.'

This is a warning parenthetically to the stubborn addressees: do not consider the Resurrection to be very far. If its time is not known, it does not mean that it will not come at all. The unseen of the heavens and the earth is in the knowledge of Allah alone. When He wills to bring it, it will come like the blink of an eye, or even quicker. Allah is competent over all things.

'And Allah has extracted you from the wombs of your mothers not knowing a thing, and He made for you hearing and vision and intellect that perhaps you would be grateful.'

That is, when a human is born, he is merely a lump of flesh, completely devoid of intellect, knowledge, power, and capability. Then, Allah grants him the powers of hearing, vision, heart, and brain. The right of these blessings is that you become grateful to Allah, but the state of most people is that upon receiving these blessings, they make them the means of showing ingratitude to God. This very theme is stated in Surah Al-Mulk: 'Say, "It is He who has produced you and made for you hearing and vision and hearts; little are you grateful."'

Regarding the Divine Signs: 'Do they not see the birds controlled in the atmosphere of the sky? None holds them except Allah. Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe.' That is, if they observe with open eyes, they would know that everything received its necessary provisions purely by the grace of God. Birds fly in the air; after all, who holds them in the air except God? It is stated in Surah Al-Mulk: 'Do they not see the birds above them with wings outspread and [sometimes] folded? None holds them except the Most Merciful. Indeed He is, of all things, Seeing.' 'Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe'—meaning that for those who want to believe, there are many signs in this. The greatest sign in this is that the Creator of this universe is Compassionate and Merciful; He provided every creature He created with all its requirements and granted it the skill to use them. There is also a sign in this that this universe is under the control of only one God, without partner; He alone creates harmony and compatibility among its opposites, making the atmosphere a playground for birds. There is also a sign in this that whatever is held in place is held only by God's holding; if He did not hold them, nothing could remain in its place. It is stated in Surah Al-Mulk: 'It is He who made the earth tame for you - so walk among its slopes and eat of His provision - and to Him is the resurrection. Do you feel secure that He who is in the heaven will not cause the earth to swallow you when suddenly it sways? Or do you feel secure that He who is in the heaven will not send against you a storm of stones? Then you would know how [severe] was My warning.'

'And Allah has made for you from your homes a place of rest and made for you from the hides of animals tents which you find light on the day of your travel and the day of your encampment; and from their wool, fur and hair is furnishing and enjoyment for a time.'

Invitation to Ponder Over One's Own Conditions: That is, you have seen that birds have their tents and shelters with them. Now, reflect on your own conditions: how in various ways Allah has blessed you with His favors. It is Allah Who made your homes a place of rest and comfort for you; when you return to your homes exhausted after the day's work, what comfort you find, and what a sense of security you feel in the darkness of the night! Then, it is God Who made the hides of animals suitable for making tents, from which you make tents that are highly light and easy for your travel and encampment; then, from their wool, fur, and hair, you prepare other furnishings and things of necessity which serve you.

'And Allah has made for you, from that which He has created, shadows and has made for you from the mountains, shelters and has made for you garments which protect you from the heat and garments which protect you from your [mutual] enemy. Thus does He complete His favor upon you that you might submit [to Him].'

Indication of Some Other Signs: And see further, it is God Who created shadows for you within His created things. He made shelters for you in the mountains, and made garments for you which protect you from the heat and the hot wind, and protect you in battles. Allah completed His favor upon you in this manner so that you do not remain in need of anyone other than God, and surrender yourselves entirely to your Lord.

In mentioning garments, the reference to protection from heat is keeping in view the primary addressees of the Qur'an—the Arabs. Moreover, in the things mentioned above, the nomadic life of the Arabs has been kept in view. To estimate the beauty of the discourse and its impact correctly, it is necessary to consider this aspect of the address.

The Blessing of Everyday Items: Note also that the things referred to above are everyday items of life with which every individual of the addressed group had to deal. By mentioning them, the Qur'an has, so to speak, drawn attention: when there is not even a single one of these things which you can attribute to anyone other than Allah, why do you worship others besides God? What is their purpose? You must surrender yourselves entirely to God alone, Who arranged even your small, day-to-day requirements.

'But if they turn away, then upon you is only clear notification.' This is directed to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that if they persist in their aversion and denial, do not exhaust yourself over them; the responsibility of their faith is not upon you, but solely to convey the truth clearly. You perform your duty and leave them to their state. They will see the end of this stubbornness of theirs.

'They recognize the favor of Allah; then they deny it, and most of them are disbelievers.' That is, it is not the case that these things do not enter their understanding; after all, who can be so devoid of intellect as not to understand that all these blessings are bestowed by Allah alone? These people are knowingly pretending to be ignorant, and most of them are ungrateful; only a few among them will believe. So leave them to their state; they will face the consequences.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 84 to 90: The Messenger is the Final Means for the Completion of Proof: The ultimate limit of the Prophet's responsibility has been clarified above. Subsequently, in connection with this, the reality is clarified: after the completion of proof through a messenger, no excuse remains for any nation to stick to their misguidance which they can present before God. Allah sent His messengers in every nation to convey the truth and complete the proof. He will make them stand as witnesses on the Day of Resurrection to testify that they conveyed Allah's message to the people. This is the very status of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) for the people of this nation. For this purpose, Allah has revealed this Book to you. An elliptical reference to the fundamental message of this Book is at the end. Read the verses:

'And [warn of] the Day when We will resurrect from every nation a witness. Then it will not be permitted to those who disbelieved [to apologize or make excuses], nor will they be asked to appease [Allah].'

'Nation' (Ummah) here refers to the nation of call to which a messenger is sent, and 'witness' (shaheed) refers to the messenger, because he is the one who bears witness to the truth against his people in the world, and he will be made to stand as a witness on the Day of Resurrection, testifying that he conveyed Allah's religion to the people. After 'Then it will not be permitted...', there is an omission in the discourse, as clear from the context—meaning that after the testimony of the messengers on the Day of Resurrection, the disbelievers will not be allowed to open their tongues for presenting excuses and apologies. 'Istictab' means asking someone to remove the cause of complaint and please him. Here, it means that on the Day of Resurrection, the disbelievers and disobedient ones will not be asked to try to please God, because at that time, the doors of effort and action will be closed, and the stage of reward and punishment will begin. 'After death there is no making amends, and after the world there is no abode except Paradise or the Fire.'

Warning to the Disbelievers: This is to the disbelievers—do not advance too far in your rebellion; remember that Day when We will raise the messenger sent to every nation as a witness, and obtain testimony from him that he conveyed Allah's message fully to the people. Then, neither will anyone be allowed to open his tongue to present excuses, nor will anyone be asked to try to please God and remove His wrath, because after the completion of proof through the messenger, no opportunity for excuses remains, and after the Resurrection, no opportunity for making amends remains.

'And when those who wronged see the punishment, it will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved.' 'Those who wronged' (alladina zalamoo) refers to those who committed polytheism, violating the rights of God and wronging their own selves. It is said that when these wrongdoers fall into the grip of God's punishment on the Day of Resurrection, neither will this punishment be lightened, nor will they receive any temporary respite from it; rather, the punishment will keep growing severer, and no break will occur in its continuity.

'And when those who associated partners see their partners, they will say, "Our Lord, these are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You." But they will throw back at them the word: "Indeed, you are liars."

Exchange Between the Polytheists and Their Partners: That is, those partners upon whom these polytheists rely today will call them liars face to face. When they see their partners, they will cry out: 'O Lord! These are the ones whom we used to call upon besides You.' The partners will instantly throw back at them the word: 'Indeed, you are liars.' Meaning: 'We had no knowledge that you called upon us. If you committed this folly and ignorance, the responsibility is entirely yours; you face it. Why are you trying to drag us into this? When did we ask you to leave God and call upon us?' Note that the polytheists' statement 'these are our partners whom we used to call upon besides You' will be, to some extent, an attempt to absolve themselves, implying that these are the actual criminals who caused our misguidance; in this light, the bitterness of the partners' response becomes clear.

'And they will show submission to Allah that Day, and lost from them is what they used to invent.' The explanation of the word 'salam' has passed under verse 28. The meaning of the verse is: when matters reach the point where those whom they worshipped throughout their lives call them liars face to face, no room will remain for them to present excuses or say anything in their defense; they will completely surrender, and those deities of theirs whom they associated as partners with God by fabricating lies against Him will all vanish.

Here, polytheism is termed as 'invention' (iftra'). The reason is that the polytheists claimed without any authority that God made so-and-so His partner. Obviously, this is an explicit slander against Allah.

'Those who disbelieved and averted [people] from the way of Allah - We will increase them in punishment over punishment for what corruption they used to cause.'

More Punishment for the Leaders of Polytheism: This points to those leaders of the disbelievers and polytheists who were not only in disbelief and polytheism themselves, but also blocked others from the way of Allah. It is said that as a punishment for this corruption of theirs, We will increase their punishment over punishment. They will have to face the punishment for their own misguidance, and according to their share in the misguidance of others, they will also have to face punishment for that.

'And [warn of] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves. And We will bring you, [O Muhammad], as a witness over these. And We have sent down to you the Book as clarification for all things and as guidance and mercy and good tidings for the Muslims.'

This subject has passed above in verse 84. The addition here is: 'And We will bring you as a witness over these'—meaning that just as We will raise a witness in every nation, We will raise you as a witness over these people. 'Over these' refers to the Arabs to whom your advent was direct, and who stand as your nation of call. Note that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was sent with two advents: his advent to the Arabs was direct, and his advent to all creation is through his nation which holds the status of Allah's witnesses on the earth, and upon whom lies the responsibility of guiding humanity until the Day of Resurrection.

The Divine Book is the Means for the Completion of Proof: 'And We have sent down to you the Book as clarification for all things.' This refers to that which is the means for bearing witness to the truth and completing the proof against people in this world. It is said: according to the requirement of this office of yours, We have revealed the Book to you, which is comprehensive and perfect for bearing witness to the truth in all aspects, and clearly explains every relevant matter so that no excuse remains for anyone to stick to his misguidance. In praise of this Book, it is further said: 'and as guidance and mercy and good tidings for the Muslims.' This is guidance, mercy, and glad tidings for those who completely surrender themselves to their Lord. It will guide them to the straight path, and those who adopt this straight path will receive the great mercy of the Compassionate, Merciful God, and this is the advance glad tiding of that great mercy.

Summary of Qur'an's Commands and Prohibitions: 'Indeed, Allah orders justice and good conduct and giving to relatives and forbids immorality and bad conduct and oppression. He admonishes you that perhaps you will be reminded.'

This great verse is the summary of all Qur'anic commands and prohibitions. The foundations of what the Qur'an commands have been clarified in it, and the bases of what it prohibits are also pointed to in it. All Qur'anic commands are based on justice, good conduct (ihsan), and spending on relatives; and its prohibitions include those things in which the spirit of corruption of immorality (fahsha'), bad conduct (munkar), and oppression (bag_y) is found. The purpose of mentioning it here is to warn those who were expending all their power in opposing the Qur'an—making them reflect on what is the teaching of that which they are opposing, and what justice and goodness they are opposing, and what evil and corruption they are supporting.

Here, only the bases of commands and prohibitions are mentioned in brief. It does not clarify what kind of commands fall under justice and good conduct, and what nature of prohibitions are included under immorality, bad conduct, or oppression. The detail of this brief mention comes ahead in the subsequent Surah—Surah Bani Israel, verses 22 to 39. Surah Bani Israel stands as the partner (mathna) of Surah An-Nahl; for this reason, it was the highly appropriate place to clarify this brief mention. To avoid repetition, we consider it suitable to clarify this brief mention there, where the components of justice, good conduct, spending, immorality, bad conduct, and oppression will be before us, making it easy to understand these principles of good and evil. There, if Allah wills, we will also clarify that there is great compatibility between this detail and the Ten Commandments of the Torah, showing that these principles of good and evil were accepted in previous religions too.

Explanation of Justice (Adl): Briefly keep in mind that 'justice' is that we pay whatever is the due right of someone upon us without any decrease, whether the possessor of the right is weak or powerful, and whether he is disliked or beloved to us.

Explanation of Good Conduct (Ihsan): Good conduct is something additional to justice. It does not merely demand the payment of the due right, but further demands that our dealing with others be generous and noble.

The Meaning of 'Giving to Relatives' (Ita'i zil-qurba): Giving to relatives is a highly important branch of good conduct. Relatives are entitled to justice and good conduct, and further, by virtue of relationship, they are entitled to additional spending; every possessor of wealth must spend generously on his relatives and family.

The Meaning of 'Immorality' (Fahsha') and 'Bad Conduct' (Munkar): 'Fahsha'' refers to open indecency and vice, such as fornication, sodomy, and other evils of this type. 'Munkar' is the opposite of 'ma'roof'. 'Ma'roof' refers to those good things which are practiced in every good society, such as hospitality, traveler-care, and other virtues of this type. 'Munkar' is its opposite, so it refers to those things which are contrary to the ma'roof and the approved ways and manners of reason and custom.

The Meaning of 'Oppression' (Bag_y): 'Bag_y' means rebellion and transgression—meaning that a person takes undue advantage of his power, influence, and strength to suppress others.

Content yourself with these brief indications here; their full detail will come in the subsequent Surah.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 91 to 95: Blame upon the Jews: In the subsequent verses, the address is to the Jews, and this address has come suddenly without taking their name, just as a speaker sometimes changes the direction of speech merely by shifting his posture. Although he does not name whom he is addressing, his statements themselves determine the addressee. The Jews at this stage were no longer neutral regarding Islam; rather, feeling threatened by it, they were busy in secret conspiracies to oppose it. They had started supporting those leaders of the Quraysh who had come out to block this call with full force. The Qur'an has blamed the Jews here for this action of theirs—showing that having bound a covenant with Allah, you have stood in the ranks of the opponents merely out of jealousy, and like that old woman who unravels her own spun thread with her own hands, you are unraveling the threads of your covenant with Allah. Do not sell Allah's covenant for a small price of the world, and do not make your oaths a means of turning people away from the truth. Read the verses:

'And fulfill the covenant of Allah when you have taken it, [O believers], and do not break oaths after their confirmation while you have made Allah, over you, a witness [i.e., security]. Indeed, Allah knows what you do.'

Command to the Jews to Fulfill the Covenant: In verse 88 above, those wicked of the Quraysh were mentioned who were expending all their power to block people from Allah's religion. Now, in connection with this, without taking their name, the Jews are addressed, because at this stage, they too had started supporting this campaign with all their trickery. Turning toward them, it is said: fulfill the covenant you bound with Allah, and do not break your oaths after confirming them and making Allah a witness and guarantor over them. Remember that whatever you are doing and whatever you will do, Allah is fully aware of it.

Keep in view that Moses (peace be upon him) presented every command of the Shariah before the entire congregation, taking a covenant of its adherence by making Allah a witness and guarantor, and people swore oaths to adhere to it. In this way, their entire Shariah held the status of a covenant between Allah and the Children of Israel. Consequently, on this basis, the Torah is called the Testament. This covenant, just as it comprises the general commands and laws of the Shariah, also includes that covenant which was taken from the Children of Israel to support and help the unlettered Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) raised from among the Children of Ishmael. Under 'Indeed, Allah orders justice...' above, we also indicated that the bases on which the Qur'an's commands and prohibitions are founded are the very bases on which the Ten Commandments of the Torah are founded. The Qur'an has reminded them of these covenants here: fulfill the covenant you bound with God, and do not break your oaths after confirming them and making Allah a guarantor and witness over them; remember that whatever you are doing today, Allah is fully aware of it, and tomorrow you will have to answer for this covenant.

'And do not be like she who untied her yarn, after it was strong, into untwisted strands, taking your oaths as [a means of] deceit between you because a nation is more numerous than another nation. Allah only tries you thereby. And He will surely make clear to you on the Day of Resurrection that over which you used to differ.'

'Ankath' is the plural of 'nikth', meaning unraveled thread or unraveled cloth.

'Dahal' means deceit, trickery, and corruption.

Indication of the Corrupt Activities of the Jews: Addressing the Jews, it is said: do not be like that woman who unties her spun yarn, after spinning and strengthening it, into untwisted strands with her own hands. That is, first you bound the covenant and confirmed it by swearing oaths, but when the time came to fulfill it, you unraveled the threads of this covenant. 'taking your oaths as [a means of] deceit between you'—meaning that you make your oaths a means of corruption, deceit, and blocking Allah's servants from His way, and uprooting their stable steps. This points to those corrupt efforts of the Jews which they were practicing to block those inclined toward Islam or to waver those who had accepted Islam. They would swear oaths to convince people that this new religion is completely contrary to the divine religion, claiming: 'Neither does any proof of it exist in our scriptures, nor did our prophets make any indication toward it.' Since the Jews possessed religious prestige, their oaths produced anxiety in the hearts of those who were not well aware of their tricks and hidden motives. 'because a nation is more numerous than another nation'—before this, 'makhafata' (out of fear of) or a word of similar meaning is often omitted.

The Actual Motive of the Jews' Activity: This uncovers the actual motive of all these corrupt activities of the Jews—showing that all your striving is out of this jealous fear that you worry lest this newly established nation exceed your nation in number and strength. This fear has made you enemies of the truth instead of its supporters; in this way, God is trying you, observing whether you become supporters of the truth or remain trapped in the net of your prejudices. Remember that those things which you have made points of difference today, Allah will clearly explain on the Day of Resurrection what the truth was and how you knowingly tried to cover it.

Comparing the Jews to an Old Woman: Although 'alladi' and 'allati' are for definite nouns, in illustrations, the use of definite nouns is sometimes intended solely to depict the situation before the eyes; for this reason, there is no need to assume a specific old woman here, but it is sufficient to assume an old woman whose image brings the entire picture before the mind's eye. Just as the Jews are compared to an old woman here, they are compared in verses 175-176 of Surah Al-A'raf to a person whom Allah blessed with His verses and teachings, but he slipped away from them, resulting in Satan overtaking him and his becoming of the deviators. After this, their illustration is given as a dog which pants whether it is chased or left alone. In that illustration too, 'alladi' is used, but we clarified in its place that it is intended solely for depicting the situation, not pointing to a specific person.

'And if Allah had willed, He could have made you [of] one religion, but He sends astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And you will surely be questioned about what you used to do.' That is, if Allah wanted to use His absolute power, He could have made everyone a single nation, but instead of compulsion, He granted you choice, and in this way He wants to try you—observing whether you use your understanding, choice, and will to adopt the path of guidance or misguidance. So, He grants the enablement of guidance to those among you who seek it, and leaves those in misguidance who choose to stick to it. We have clarified this reality in various places—showing that God's will is under His wisdom. 'And you will surely be questioned about what you used to do'—meaning: remember that He has granted you intellect to distinguish between guidance and misguidance, and has also completed His proof against you through His prophets and messengers. For this reason, a Day will come when every single one of you will be questioned about what you used to do, and then you will receive reward or punishment according to your deeds.

'And do not make your oaths [a means of] deceit between you, lest a foot slip after it was firmly planted, and you taste evil [i.e., punishment] for what you averted [people] from the way of Allah, and you will have a great punishment.'

Oaths were the Jews' Means of Corruption: The weapon which the Jews used most to block people from Islam was their oaths. Untruthful people, firstly, swear oaths frequently due to their psychological weakness; moreover, what proof did they possess which they could present against Islam and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)? They relied solely on oaths. On their strength, they tried to waver those whose steps were firmly planted in Islam, and block those who were inclined toward it. Since these people claimed to be the heirs of the previous religion and prophets, they, under the veil of their religious prestige, tried to convince people by swearing oaths: 'This new religion and Prophet have no relation to the previous religions and prophets, and this Prophet is lying in his reference to the previous religions and prophets.' 'and you taste evil [i.e., punishment] for what you averted...'—'soo'' means evil and bad. Here, 'soo'' refers to the evil consequence, i.e., punishment. Since divine punishment is the result of people's own deeds, the consequence of the action is expressed through the verb itself. The meaning is: do not do this—lest by your deceitful oaths, someone's steps firmly planted on the truth slip, and you face the punishment for your crime of blocking people from the way of Allah. If this happened, you would face a great punishment; you were made witnesses to the truth, and if you yourselves tried to block people from the truth, you would face a highly severe punishment.

'And do not exchange the covenant of Allah for a small price. Indeed, what is with Allah is best for you, if you only knew.'

'Ishtira'' in the sense of Preferring and Exchanging: We have discussed the word 'ishtira'' in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, and also under verse 77 of Surah Aal-Imran. When things are exchanged for things, as was common in ancient times, everything can be a commodity (mabee') as well as a price (thaman). For this reason, the 'ishtira'' of a thing does not mean buying it in the sense we use 'buying', but meant exchanging it. For this reason, the word 'ishtira'' is used in the sense of exchanging, and then, progressing from this, in the sense of preferring. 'Small price' (thaman qaleel) refers to the world and its provisions, and 'what is with Allah' (ma indallah) refers to the reward of the Hereafter. In comparison to the reward of the Hereafter, no matter how great the amount of worldly provisions is obtained, it is insignificant and trivial because it is temporary and fleeting, while the reward of the Hereafter is eternal and permanent. It is said: do not sell Allah's covenant for a trivial worldly benefit. The reward with Allah for standing by His covenant is far better than the benefits of this world, if you only knew.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 96 to 105: Reply to Some Objections of the Opponents: First, there is a threat to the opponents of the truth, and advice of patience and steadfastness to those Muslims who were surrounded by the opponents, along with the glad tiding of its good end. Then, there is an instruction to keep oneself safe from the whispers of Satan during the recitation of the Qur'an, and a reply to some of the objections which the Jews had spread to turn people away from the Qur'an and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and which the Quraysh had also started repeating without understanding. Read the verses:

Warning to the Opponents, Glad Tiding to the Believers: 'Whatever you have will end, but what is with Allah is remaining. And We will surely give those who were patient their reward according to the best of what they used to do.' The first part of this verse is a warning to the opponents, and the second part is a glad tiding for the believers who were targeted with various spiritual and physical hardships while surrounded by the opponents at that time. Addressing the opponents, it is said: the worldly provisions for the love of which you are opposing the truth will end one day; what remains is the reward which will be received in return for the deeds performed in this world. So hear: We will surely give this reward to those who are bearing hardships in Our way today and are firm on the truth. This reward of theirs will be far better in proportion to their deeds.

This is the detail of the aforementioned glad tiding. It is stated that whoever does a righteous deed, whether male or female, while being a believer, We will surely cause them to live a good and pure life, and We will certainly pay them their reward according to the best of what they used to do. This promise of causing them to live a good and pure life is both in terms of religion and the world. Those who determine to remain firm on faith and live a life of righteous deeds do face trials. The devils among jinn and humans try to harass them and put them into complexities, and they receive respite for this, but there is a specific limit to this respite. In this way, Allah tests the patience and steadfastness of His servants and knows well what the limit of this test should be. Consequently, as soon as that limit is complete, Allah does not allow the devils among jinn and humans to advance beyond it. After passing through these tests, the believers become stronger and stronger in their faith. The purity of their lives and their peace of mind and tranquility grow daily, and if they face death in this path, they welcome it with a smiling face. Those who are unaware of the strength and sweetness of faith cannot estimate this spiritual kingdom of the believers.

This promise is explicitly mentioned in the verse for both men and women. Apparently, this explicit mention was not necessary, but it has a specific context: in this era, just as Muslim men were passing through highly severe tests to remain steadfast in their faith, many women were also putting their lives at stake to save their faith, and their test—being the weaker gender—was even more severe than that of the men. Here, the Qur'an has consoled and encouraged them by explicitly mentioning women alongside men—showing that if they have determined to live a life of faith and righteous deeds, Allah will surely cause them to live a pure life. The devils cannot deprive them of this blessing; Allah will not give them the opportunity for it.

Instruction to Seek Refuge from Satanic Temptations: 'So when you recite the Quran, [first] seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the expelled [from His mercy].' The word 'recitation' (qira'at) is general here. That is, whether this reading is for one's own recitation or to convey and recite the Qur'an to people, under both conditions, one must seek Allah's refuge from the accursed Satan before reading it.

This verse is an introduction to the replies to those objections which the Jews raised against the Qur'an, and by raising which their purpose was to make those who believed in the Qur'an or were inclined toward it skeptical and turned away. The Qur'an has replied to these objections in verses 101 to 103 ahead, and before this reply, He instructed as an introduction: when you read the Qur'an or recite it for the call of truth, seek Allah's refuge from the temptations of the accursed Satan before it. This instruction was given because, in reality, the actual enemy of the Qur'an is Satan. When the Qur'an is read or recited, he stands up to whisper against it. He tries to cast whispers into the hearts of its readers and listeners, and he makes those people his partners in this campaign of blocking from Allah's path who stand as his allies and friends among humans. This seeking of refuge (ta'awwudh) is like an amulet that protects a person from the temptations and whispering of all such wicked ones and devils. Its effect depends on the state of a person's heart. If a person merely repeats the words of refuge with the tongue, it holds no major effect; but those from the depths of whose hearts this prayer emerges, obtain from it such strength and power which becomes a major support in fighting Satan and his allies. This fighting is not harmful to one under any circumstances; rather, through it, a person's own capabilities are highlighted. This is the blessing of seeking refuge: if a person has its support, he does not suffer defeat at the hands of Satan and his agents.

Commentary on Verses 99 and 100: 'Indeed, he has no authority over those who have believed and otherwise rely upon their Lord. His authority is only over those who take him as an ally and those who through him associate partners with Allah.' 'Sultan' means control, strength, and authority.

Devils Have No Power Over the Believers: This is a further explanation of the above subject—showing that although Satan and his agents have indeed received this respite to tempt and mislead people, they do not possess control and authority over people to force them into misguidance. Satan's strength works only upon those who make him their friend and associate partners with Allah; he has no power over those who have believed and rely upon their Lord. This verse provides assurance: no matter how dangerous Satan's temptations are, the person who wants to remain safe from them can keep himself safe, and the way to do this is to keep strong faith in God and rely upon His grace and support even in the most difficult circumstances. Only he suffers defeat at the hands of Satan who develops friendship with him and who possesses some pollution of polytheism within him.

'And when We substitute a verse in place of a verse - and Allah is most knowing of what He sends down - they say, 'You are only a fabricator.' But most of them do not know.' 'Verse' (Ayah) here refers to a legal command (hukm shari'i).

This is an example of those objections which the Jews raised against the Qur'an to cast whispers into people's hearts. It is stated that when We replace one command with another, they say: 'You are merely a fabricator. You make up a thing from your own side and attribute it to God to impose yourself on people. Otherwise, what is the meaning of God making a law at one time and then replacing that law Himself with another law?'

This points to those commands regarding prohibition and permission, and the Sabbath, which are referred to in verses 114 to 116 and 124 of this Surah. When the Qur'an ended the sanctity of the Sabbath for this nation and gave those commands regarding eating and drinking which were based on the religion of Abraham rather than the religion of the Jews or the polytheists, the Jews began to make a hue and cry: 'Look, this person on one hand accepts our shariah as a divine shariah, and on the other hand, he replaces its commands with different commands. If this person were a messenger of God, how could he change the commands given by God? For this reason, in our view, he is not a messenger of God but a fabricator.' This point was spoken by the Jews, but it was also repeated blindly by those leaders of the Quraysh who considered every statement spoken against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) as their support. 'And Allah is most knowing of what He sends down'—this is the Qur'an's reply to the aforementioned objection, showing that God alone knows best the wisdom and interest behind the things He sends down. 'But most of them do not know'—most people do not know these wisdoms and interests.

This point is stated here in brief. The details of this brief point will come in the subsequent verses. We make a few indications here solely to make the understanding of the sequence of speech ahead easy. This reply of the Qur'an is based on two principles:

The Wisdom of Gradualness in Shariah: First, God has kept order and gradualness (tadreej) in revealing His shariah so that the steps of those who believe may remain firmly fixed in the shariah. This order and gradualness has required changes in commands under certain conditions, which is entirely based on the interest of the believers. Second, the foundation of this nation is on the religion of Abraham, and the religion of Abraham was completely separate from the religion of the Jews and the religion of the polytheists. In it, only those things are forbidden which were forbidden in the religion of Abraham. These things were also originally forbidden in the religion of the Jews, but later they made additions through their innovations, which Allah kept imposed upon them as a punishment; but since the foundation of this nation is on the religion of Abraham, those additions were abolished in it.

'Say, 'The Pure Spirit [i.e., Gabriel] has brought it down from your Lord in truth to make firm those who believe and as guidance and good tidings for the Muslims.'

'Pure Spirit' (Rooh-ul-Qudus) refers to Gabriel, the trustworthy; and 'truth' (Al-Haqq) refers to that pure and unadulterated religion which has been revealed from Allah, cleansed of all pollutions of the Jews, Christians, and polytheists.

This is an instruction to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): give reply to those who call you a fabricator: 'I am not fabricating what I am presenting from my own side to attribute it to God; rather, Gabriel, the close angel of God, has brought it down upon me from my Lord, and this is entirely the truth, completely clean of every pollution of the devils and corrupters.' 'To make firm those who believe'—that is, Allah has revealed the religion clean of all pollutions so that He may make those who believe firm on the truth, keeping them completely safe from slips and misguidance. Here, it must also be remembered that if any change has occurred in some parts of the religion from Allah's side, it occurred—as we indicated above—due to the wisdom of gradualness, and the purpose behind it was to make people deeply rooted in the religion. If the entire religion had been revealed at once, it would have been highly difficult for people, and their steps could not have been firmly fixed in the religion. 'And as guidance and good tidings for the Muslims'—meaning, let the opponents raise whatever trials they want from it, but for those who are ready to say 'we believe and confirm' to every word of God, this is guidance in terms of its beginning and glad tidings in terms of its end.

'And We certainly know that they say, 'It is only a human who teaches him.' The tongue of the one they refer to is foreign, and this Quran is a clear Arabic language.' 'Wa laqad na'lamu' is originally 'wa laqad kunna na'lamu'—meaning, sometimes the incomplete verbs (af'aal naqisah) are omitted before the present untensed verb. This style of expression has been discussed in detail under Surah Al-Baqarah and other places.

This refers to another mischief of the opponents which they raised to make people skeptical of the Qur'an: they said that calling this discourse a divine revelation and brought by an angel is merely an imposition. 'It is only a human who teaches him, and he recites it to us to impress us that God has revealed it to him through Gabriel.' No specific person's name is mentioned here, only the clarification of his being non-Arab (A'jami) is given. The commentators have mentioned more than one name here, among which the name of Salman the Persian is also present. It is possible that the opponents pointed toward him. The Qur'an is not concerned with their names, but with the objection, and He has replied to it. The introduction of the reply is raised in such a way that the absurdity of this objection becomes clear. Because in the style of 'We certainly know', it is implicit that We have been constantly hearing this nonsense of the objectors, but We paid no attention to it due to its absurdity. After this, He stated: if these foolish ones do not possess the ability to recognize the meanings and realities of the Qur'an, they should have at least considered the power and majesty of its expression and the matchless eloquence of its language, which would have made the absurdity of their objection clear to them—how can a crooked, non-Arab expression be compared to this clear Arabic washed in the fountain of Kauthar? After all, who can be so foolish as not to differentiate between water splashed from the mouth and the rain from heaven?

This statement is made by way of concession (tanazzul). The meaning is: we cannot expect these people who cannot differentiate between pearls and pebbles to reach the meanings and realities of the Qur'an; but they take great pride in their own eloquence, so they should have at least considered its language—forgetting a non-Arab, it is not within the power of any of their own poets or orators to present such a discourse. To the extent that there is a difference of heaven and earth between the Prophet's own speech and this discourse.

'Indeed, those who do not believe in the verses of Allah - Allah will not guide them, and for them is a painful punishment.' That is, the path of faith and guidance is solely to believe in the verses of Allah, because guidance is only that which comes from Allah; so those who are not ready to believe in the verses of Allah cannot receive Allah's guidance. They have closed the doors of guidance upon themselves, and ahead of them is only a painful punishment.

'They only invent falsehood who do not believe in the verses of Allah, and it is those who are the liars.'

The Absurdity of Those Who Consider the Prophet a Fabricator: It has passed in verse 101 above that these people consider the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a fabricator. Pointing to this, He stated: our Prophet is not a fabricator; rather, the fabricators are those who do not believe in the verses of Allah—which are the sole means of knowing Allah's guidance—yet they have fabricated a religion and call it the religion of Allah. He stated that this is a completely lie and fabrication, and its fabricators are the actual liars.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 106 to 111: Exhortation to Patience and Steadfastness for the Oppressed Muslims: In the subsequent verses, attention is directed toward those Muslims who were targets of the tyranny and oppression of the enemies of truth. Addressing them, He exhorts them to patience and steadfastness, while also threatening those with divine punishment who become turned away from Allah's religion out of fear of the opponents. In this connection, there is also an indication toward those Muslims who had to migrate due to being harassed by the atrocities of the enemies of truth. They are praised, and there is a glad tiding of forgiveness and pleasure for them. Read the verse:

'Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief... except for one who is forced while his heart is secure in faith. But those who [willingly] open their breasts to disbelief, upon them is wrath from Allah, and for them is a great punishment.'

Awareness for the Oppressed Muslims: This is awareness for those who were targets of the disbelievers' atrocities in that era. He stated that those who are in the clutches of the enemies of truth have permission to utter some word of compromise with the tongue to save their lives, while their hearts remain firmly established on faith. But there is no permission to use this force and oppression as an excuse to open their breasts to disbelief. Regarding those who do so, He stated that upon them is the wrath of Allah and a very great punishment. Their coming to faith once is clear proof that the correctness and truth of this matter had become clear to them. After this, while there remains room to adopt a strategy for one's protection which outwardly appears contrary to faith, there remains no room to abandon it entirely and make disbelief one's cover. He stated that those who choose this path of ease will face God's wrath and a great punishment.

'That is because they preferred the worldly life over the Hereafter and that Allah does not guide the disbelieving people.' This explains the reason why such people face Allah's wrath. He stated that this is because they have preferred their worldly life over the Hereafter and handed themselves over to disbelief, unconcerned by God's seizure. Allah opens the path of His guidance only for those who make His guidance their goal under all circumstances; He does not guide those who, becoming panicked by difficulties, make disbelief their refuge.

'Those are the ones over whose hearts and hearing and vision Allah has sealed, and it is those who are the heedless.' He stated that those who, after seeing the light of faith once, close their eyes to it merely for their worldly interests—Allah seals the hearts, hearing, and vision of such people, and they are completely deprived of the enablement to receive guidance. Such people neither reflect on the actual reality themselves, nor listen to the words of any other sensible person, nor open their eyes to gain insight. This points to that divine sunnah regarding guidance and misguidance which has been explained under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah. 'And it is those who are the heedless'—meaning that the actual heedless ones are these people, because their hearts, hearing, and vision have all been shut down. There remains no way for any ray of insight to enter them from any side.

'Without doubt, in the Hereafter they will be the losers.' 'La jarama' means 'without doubt' and 'surely'. Originally it is used for emphasizing a matter, but when the context requires, the force of an oath is also produced within it.

This describes the necessary consequence of what has been explained above—showing that those who show such disregard for the Hereafter for the sake of their world will be the most deprived and disappointed ones in the Hereafter.

Glad Tiding for the Brave of the Migration to Abyssinia: 'Then, indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they had been compelled and then fought and were patient - indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful.' After describing the end of the cowardly and weak-hearted, this mentions those brave and selfless ones who suffered all kinds of calamities at the hands of the disbelievers for the sake of their religion, yet were not ready to abandon it. 'Migration' here points to the migration to Abyssinia, and 'fighting and patience' refers to those sacrifices they showed in remaining steadfast on the religion. He stated that those who protected their faith by facing these life-threatening calamities—God will overlook their slips and shortcomings and bless them with His mercy.

Reminder of the Hereafter: 'On the Day when every soul will come disputing [i.e., pleading] for itself, and every soul will be fully compensated for what it did, and they will not be wronged.' This is assurance of that Day when everyone will be presented before Allah, and He will decide everyone's cases with perfect justice. He stated: 'On the Day when every soul will come pleading for itself'—on that Day, every soul will be in a state of self-concern; no one will be able to act as an advocate for another, nor will anyone stand as an intercessor for another. Whatever anyone did, the result of all of it will stand before him. Everyone will be dealt with with perfect justice; no injustice will be done to anyone.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 112 to 124: An Example of a Town for the Quraysh: In the subsequent verses, a town is presented as an example to warn the Quraysh—showing that if they do not change their path, their end can be the same as that of the people of this town. Moreover, they are directed: do not designate things as prohibited and permitted from your own side. Eat and use the permitted and pure things that Allah has provided for you, and do not prohibit them under your polytheistic superstitions. Under this, reference is also made to the reality that only those things were forbidden for the Jews which are forbidden in this nation, but due to their rebellion, they prohibited certain things upon themselves, and they now attribute their prohibition to the religion of Abraham, whereas Abraham had nothing to do with Judaism, Christianity, or the religion of the polytheists. His religion was completely separate from all of them, and that is the religion which you have been directed to follow. Respecting the Sabbath was also related to the Jews; it had nothing to do with the religion of Abraham. Read the verses:

'And Allah presents an example: a city which was safe and secure, its provision coming to it in abundance from every place, but it denied the favors of Allah. So Allah made it taste the envelope of hunger and fear for what they had been doing.'

'Qaryah' refers to a central town which has surrounding villages and territories. No specific town is named here, but the reference is to Sheba. The context is proof that the reference is to Sheba; the example of this town was presented before the people of Makkah in detail in several places. See, for example, verses 15 to 20 of Surah Saba. That town had great favor and blessings of Allah upon it: there were rows of evergreen gardens on both sides, a river, and springs; but its inhabitants showed ingratitude and undervalued His blessings, so Allah's punishment appeared in the form of an unstoppable flood and destroyed all of them.

'So Allah made it taste the envelope of hunger and fear.' In this, there is an omission according to the high style of Arabic eloquence. If this omission is made explicit, the entire statement would be: 'So Allah made it taste the taste of hunger and clothed it in the garment of fear.' That is, as a punishment for their ingratitude, Allah made them taste hunger and clothed them in the garment of fear and danger in place of peace and security.

'And there had certainly come to them a messenger from among themselves, but they denied him; so the punishment seized them while they were wrongdoers.'

That is, a messenger from among themselves came to warn them, but they denied him; as a result, Allah's punishment seized them, and Allah did not do any injustice to them, but they wronged their own selves because they prepared its causes themselves through their ingratitude.

'Then eat of what Allah has provided for you, [which is] lawful and good. And be grateful for the favor of Allah, if it is Him that you worship.'

Recalling the end of the people of Sheba to the people of Makkah, they are warned: eat and use the lawful and pure things that Allah has provided for you, and offer gratitude to Him alone for His blessings, if you are His worshippers. Keep in view that there was a major similarity between the people of Sheba and the people of Makkah. Read the words used above for the people of Sheba, then read these words used in praise of the Sanctuary of Makkah: 'Have We not established for them a safe sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things as provision from Us?' (Al-Qasas: 57); then the similarity between both will become clear. Just as the people of Sheba enjoyed a life of peace and comfort, the people of Makkah also enjoyed a life of peace and security. Being the center of the Kaaba, this city was a city of peace; no one had the courage to attack the secure city (Balad-ul-Ameen). Trade caravans from the entire country came here without fear and became a source of prosperity for the inhabitants of this city; the trade caravans of the people of Makkah themselves traveled without fear in every season, and far from harming them, the bedouin tribes along the way provided them with escorts. They are the servants and guardians of the House of Allah; the requirement of these blessings was that they should benefit from them and remain grateful to Allah, but they fell into polytheism and offered the sacrifice of the animals provided by Allah at the altars of their assumed partners, and prohibited and permitted things from their own side under their polytheistic superstitions, thereby committing the same crime of ingratitude that brought the people of Sheba to their fate.

To understand the correct meaning of 'if it is Him that you worship,' remember that the polytheists of Makkah—despite being involved in all the absurdities of polytheism—claimed that they worshipped only God. In support of the worship of idols, they had created this argument: 'We worship them only so they may bring us closer to Allah; we do not worship them except that they may bring us near to Allah in position.' They assumed that these idols were highly favored and close to God; if they remained pleased, they would intercede with God to secure whatever their worshippers wanted. Under this assumption, they offered gifts and offerings to them, and let loose animals in their names which attained a status of sanctity, so that neither was it permitted to use their milk and meat, nor could they be used for riding. The Qur'an has removed their misunderstanding here: if the claim is indeed the worship of God, then offer gratitude to Him alone for His blessings—neither attribute His blessings to others, nor offer sacrifices to them, nor prohibit or permit things in their names.

'He has only forbidden to you dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.'

Among animals, only dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that animal which has been slaughtered in the name of other than Allah are forbidden to you. Even in this, there is this concession: if any person's life is at risk due to hunger, he can save his life by eating any of these things to the minimum extent necessary to survive. Under such conditions, God will forgive him and show mercy to him. 'Neither desiring [it] nor transgressing'—this is the sole condition attached to it: he should neither desire this forbidden thing from his heart, nor exceed the minimum limit necessary to survive; that is, he should not use necessity as an excuse to indulge in the forbidden thing. For further details, take a look at what has been written under verse 3 of Surah Al-Ma'idah and verse 145 of Surah Al-An'am.

'And do not say about what your tongues assert of falsehood, 'This is lawful and this is unlawful,' to invent falsehood against Allah. Indeed, those who invent falsehood against Allah will not succeed.'

That is, the right to make things lawful and unlawful belongs to Allah alone; no one else has the right to start declaring things lawful or unlawful without any divine authority, merely based on their own assumptions. This is a lie and fabrication against Allah, because you attribute to Him what He has not said; and remember, those who invent falsehood against Allah will never succeed. 'About what your tongues assert of falsehood'—meaning, those things regarding which you have only the false and baseless claims of your own tongues, and you possess no proof from God in their support.

'To invent falsehood against Allah'—meaning, declaring things lawful and unlawful from one's own side is no simple matter; ultimately, it leads to fabricating lies against Allah, which is among the most severe crimes.

'[It is but] a brief enjoyment, and they will have a painful punishment.'

This explains 'will not succeed' (la yuflihoon)—showing that for those who invent falsehood against Allah, there is only this brief life of a few days in this world and its comfort, after which they will face an eternal, painful punishment.

'And to those who are Jews We have forbidden that which We relate to you before. And We did not wrong them, but they were wronging themselves.'

That is, originally only those things were forbidden for the Jews which have been mentioned in verse 115 above, but then they prohibited certain things upon themselves from their own side, which were then forbidden upon them as a punishment for their rebellion. God did not do any injustice to them, but they wronged their own selves. This has been explained under verse 160 of Surah An-Nisa; those who seek details should take a look at it.

'Then, indeed your Lord, to those who did evil in ignorance and thereafter repented and reformed - indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful.'

This is a glad tiding following the warnings above. Addressing the Prophet, it is said: those who—despite God's clear guidance—have been involved in mistakes, ignorance, and prejudices until now, the path of salvation is still open for them. Your Lord will forgive them and show mercy to them who did evil in ignorance and thereafter repented and reformed. The meaning is: if they repent and reform after this call of yours, the opportunity to enter God's mercy and deserve His forgiveness is still open to those who disobeyed God in the period of darkness that passed before your advent. Only the doomed will fail to benefit from this opportunity.

'Indeed, Abraham was a leader, obedient to Allah, a monotheist, and he was not of those who associate partners with Him.' The Jews, Christians, and polytheists all used the name of Abraham (peace be upon him) as an authority in support of their innovations, and each group claimed that they followed the way of Abraham and were following the religion of Abraham in matters of prohibition and permission. The Christians—who went to the extent of permitting even swine—were also involved in this madness; and what can be said of the polytheists of Makkah—they were, in their own assumption, the actual followers of the religion of Abraham. All of them, instead of evaluating their own conduct, criticized the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), claiming that he had deviated from the way of Abraham and was not following his regulations in matters of prohibition and permission, but had created a separate path of his own. The Qur'an answered all of them: Abraham was a separate nation (ummah); he was obedient to Allah, turned away from all falsehood, and was not among the polytheists. Similarly, in Surah Aal-Imran: 'Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was one pure of faith, a Muslim.'

The word 'ummah' (nation/leader) on one hand expresses the uniqueness of Abraham—showing that he belonged to none of the Jews, Christians, or polytheists, but was a monotheist and Muslim completely separate from all of them; and on the other hand, it points to that comprehensiveness and central position which Allah bestowed upon him, because of which he was not merely an individual but was the founder of a great nation.

'[He was] grateful for His favors. He chose him and guided him to a straight path.'

This is a further praise of Abraham—showing that he offered gratitude to Allah alone for His blessings, and was not like you who show ingratitude to God by attributing His blessings to idols and altars. The blessing of this gratitude of his was that God selected him for the office of prophethood and guided him to a straight path.

'And We gave him good in this world, and indeed, in the Hereafter he will be among the righteous.'

That is, in this world, Allah granted him wealth and honor, to the extent that his status became like that of a ruler, and in the Hereafter too, he will obtain his rank and position among the righteous.

'Then We revealed to you, [O Muhammad], to follow the religion of Abraham, inclining toward truth; and he was not of those who associate partners with Him.'

That is, all these claimants to the religion of Abraham—the Jews, Christians, and polytheists—are completely untruthful; however, We revealed to you to follow the religion of Abraham, so your religion is the religion of Abraham.

'The Sabbath was only appointed for those who differed over it. And indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ.'

This is an answer to an objection of the Jews. One of the objections the Jews raised against the Prophet and the Muslims was that these people do not respect the Sabbath. The Qur'an replied: respecting the Sabbath is not a part of the religion of Abraham, but the Sabbath was appointed only for the Jews, and they too differed over it and destroyed its sanctity. Now, the decision of these differences will be made on the Day of Resurrection. The differences they have regarding the rules and commands of the Sabbath are very long; there is no room for us to go into them here.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 125 to 128: Conclusion of the Surah: The subsequent verses serve as the conclusion of the Surah, in which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims are directed to remain firm on calling to Allah's religion and face the difficulties of the path with patience and steadfastness. At the same time, the way to obtain patience is shown, and the glad tiding is given that Allah's company is only for His God-fearing servants, and the success of the end is their share. Read the verses:

Wisdom and Good Instruction: 'Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good instruction, and argue with them in a way that is best. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of who is [rightly] guided.' 'Wisdom' (Hikmah) here refers to proofs and arguments, and 'good instruction' (Maw'izah Hasanah) refers to affectionate warning and reminder. In calling to religion, these two things stand as the primary principles. Whatever a person says, he should say in the light of proof and argument, and the style should not be of imposing oneself but should reflect his sincere desire for the other's well-being and sympathy, so that instead of becoming repelled, the addressee is inclined to hear his words and reflect on them. Although stubborn people do not soften even with this, this is the sole way of goodness and blessing.

Instruction to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Companions: The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and through him his companions, are directed to call to the path of Allah with wisdom and good instruction. This instruction was needed because in this era, as clear from the previous verses, the attitude of the opponents had created a highly charged environment, and now the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was also not alone, but a group of believers had stepped into the field of action with him. Under such conditions, it was not unlikely that—becoming fed up with the attitude of the opponents—the Muslims too might adopt some harsh attitude, affecting the temperament of the call. To protect this beauty of the call, this instruction was given: in calling to religion, steps must not deviate from the straight path of wisdom and good instruction.

The Wisdom of Propagation: 'And argue with them in a way that is best.' In the work of calling, discussion and conversation cannot be avoided, but even in this, this condition has been attached: use the best method for it. If the opponent adopts an provocative attitude, one should not be influenced by his attitude to return a blow for a blow, but should maintain one's refinement under all circumstances. In Surah-Bani Israel, this reality is explained thus: 'And tell My servants to say that which is best. Indeed, Satan induces [dissension] among them. Indeed Satan is ever, to mankind, a clear enemy.' 'Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of who is [rightly] guided'—meaning, in such circumstances, leave the matter to Allah; He is well aware of the astray, and is also well aware of those who are guided. He will deal with everyone as they deserve. The responsibility of the caller is limited solely to conveying the call; if he has performed his duty correctly, he is absolved of his responsibility. As to who chooses the path of misguidance and who chooses guidance, all of that relates to Allah and must be left to Him.

'And if you punish [them, O believers], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient.'

In 'punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed,' the style of correspondence (mushakalat) is maintained, an example of which is: 'recompense them as they have dealt.' 'As they have dealt' means 'as they did.' Similarly, its meaning is: if you want to take retribution, take only as much retribution as was committed against you. There must be no imbalance between the crime and its punishment. It must not be that you rise to return a stone for a brick. Moreover, avoiding forbidden things is necessary for a Muslim under all circumstances. If the opponent abuses us, we cannot abuse him in response. We can respond only while remaining within our recognized moral limits. 'But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient'—since this stage is delicate, not everyone can maintain limits correctly, and in the event of not maintaining them, the limits and restrictions imposed by Islam will be violated and the work of calling is also feared to suffer; for this reason, it is primary to show patience. There is great blessing in it for those who show patience; the goodness and blessing present in it is not present in taking retribution.

Keep in view that these instructions relate to that era when Muslims existed as individuals, surrounded by their opponents and targets of their high-handedness. This does not relate to that era when a regular, organized government of Muslims was established. The limits of action and powers of a regular, organized Islamic government are different from this. Their details have passed in the previous Surahs, particularly Surah Al-Anfal and Bara'at, and further details will come in the Madani Surahs ahead.

'And be patient, [O Muhammad], and your patience is not but through Allah. And do not grieve over them and do not be in distress from what they conspire.'

Although the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in terms of words, in terms of meaning—as clear from the previous verse—it is to all Muslims; it is as if through you, all Muslims are being directed. This is both the instruction to show patience and the way to obtain patience. He stated: 'and your patience is not but through Allah'—meaning, patience cannot be obtained except through connection with Allah. Whoever wants to obtain the station of patience must increase his connection with Allah as much as possible. The medium of connection with God is His remembrance, and the highest form of remembrance is prayer; for this reason, in other places in the Qur'an, emphasis is placed on maintaining prayer, particularly Tahajjud, alongside patience. 'And do not grieve over them and do not be in distress from what they conspire'—meaning, neither grieve over the state of these doomed ones, nor be anxious and worried by those plots they are conspiring day and night.

The Promise of Support and Glad Tiding of the Dominance of Islam: 'Indeed, Allah is with those who fear Him and those who are doers of good.' This promise of support is ultimately the glad tiding of the dominance of Islam and the Muslims. He stated that Allah is with those who fear Him and those who do good. 'Muhsinoon' (doers of good) refers to those who perform their worship and obedience to their Lord as if they are seeing Him with their own eyes. He stated that these are the ones who possess the company of Allah. The meaning is: when the company of Allah is possessed by them, the success of the final end must surely be their share.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. And our last call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
